result.question: General research on speed listening for a sighted person: evidence-based training methods and protocols, the realistic sighted comprehension ceiling in syllables/second and words/minute, throughput tricks that raise information rate without raising syllable rate (pause trimming, content-aware/variable-rate compression, chunking, silent gaps to drain the phonological buffer), tools/players exceeding Audible's 3.5x cap, and practitioner/community knowledge — with all 'x' multipliers translated against both a slow-narration (~150 wpm) and normal conversational baseline, and an honest assessment of whether 7x and 10x are physically achievable given documented ceilings (~22 syll/sec trained late-blind, ~11-16 syll/sec untrained sighted).
result.summary: The question spans five linked sub-topics (training protocols, the sighted ceiling, throughput-without-rate tricks, tools beyond 3.5x, and practitioner lore) anchored to a hard physiological-limits question. I assign one query per sub-topic, biasing toward the highest-signal source type for each: peer-reviewed psycholinguistics/auditory-perception for the ceiling and buffer-drain mechanics, accessibility/screen-reader research and forums for trained-listener rates and tools, audio-engineering docs for compression/re-encoding, and enthusiast communities for practitioner methods. Together they cover broad-primary, academic, technical-tooling, contrarian-limits, and practitioner angles without overlap.

angles.label: academic — comprehension ceiling & sighted vs blind limits
angles.query: maximum speech comprehension rate syllables per second trained blind vs sighted listeners time-compressed speech psycholinguistics
angles.rationale: Targets peer-reviewed evidence (e.g. Trouvain, Dietrich, Moos; blind ultrafast-speech studies) to fix the documented ~22 syll/s blind ceiling and the ~11-16 syll/s sighted range, grounding the honest 7x/10x feasibility assessment.

angles.label: training — protocols and adaptation gains
angles.query: training to improve time-compressed speech comprehension perceptual learning adaptation rate practice protocol fast speech listening
angles.rationale: Surfaces perceptual-adaptation and training-study literature on how quickly listeners adapt to accelerated speech, plateau/overload effects, and how long measurable gains take — the evidence base for a ramp/practice schedule.

angles.label: throughput tricks — pause trimming, variable-rate, buffer drain
angles.query: increase listening throughput without faster playback silence trimming variable-rate speech compression content-aware pause removal phonological loop
angles.rationale: Covers raising information density without raising syllable rate: silence/pause trimming, content-aware compression, chunking, and inserting gaps to drain phonological short-term storage — including the buffer/gap-timing mechanics.

angles.label: tools — players beyond 3.5x and re-encoding
angles.query: audiobook player faster than 3.5x speed Rubber Band SoundTouch time stretch re-encode screen reader TTS high speed playback app
angles.rationale: Finds custom players, NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver high-rate TTS, and Rubber Band/SoundTouch re-encoding workflows that exceed Audible's 3.5x cap and support pause-trimming/variable-rate techniques.

angles.label: practitioner — speed-listening enthusiasts & screen-reader power users
angles.query: speed listening enthusiasts how to listen at 4x 5x 6x audiobooks blind screen reader power users fast speech reddit forum tips
angles.rationale: Captures community/practitioner knowledge from r/audiobooks, blind/NVDA/JAWS user forums, and speed-listening blogs on realistic achievable speeds, ramping habits, and tool configurations.

results.title: Strategic Pauses Relieve Listeners from the Effort of Listening to Fast Speech (O'Leary et al., 2023, Trends in Hearing)
results.snippet: The single most on-target source for the 'buffer drain' angle. Demonstrates the core mechanism: fast/time-compressed speech deprives the listener of processing time normally present at natural rates; inserting LENGTHENED silent gaps at linguistically salient points (clause and sentence boundaries) lets processing 'catch up' and restores recall accuracy to near-normal levels. Crucially, pauses at RANDOM points unrelated to clause structure yield little/no improvement, so gap PLACEMENT (not just total silence) is what matters. Frames it via data-limited vs resource-limited processing - directly relevant to draining the phonological-storage buffer between high-rate bursts. Implication for the user: you can keep syllable rate high during speech and recover comprehension by reinserting boundary-aligned gaps.

results.title: Temporal fine structure and restoring syllabic rhythm in time-compressed speech (PMC)
results.snippet: Shows intelligibility of heavily time-compressed speech can be RESTORED by 'repackaging' - reinserting silences between syllables to rebuild the original rhythm. Supports the theoretical claim that reduced intelligibility comes from a MISALIGNMENT between the rate informative events are expected and the rate they actually occur; reinserted gaps counteract that. This is the mechanistic backbone for 'inserting silent gaps to drain the buffer' - and it implies a syllable-rate ceiling that pure speedup cannot beat, but rhythm-restoration can partly rescue.

results.title: Overcast 'Smart Speed' - Marco Arment
results.snippet: Primary real-world tool implementing the throughput-without-higher-syllable-rate idea. Smart Speed analyzes the waveform in real time and removes dead air / shortens silences WITHOUT pitch-shifting voice, then smooths transitions so there is no audible glitch. The key insight for the user: this lets you keep the actual speech (syllable) rate LOWER while raising overall pace - finishing faster while the narrator still sounds slower. Typical real savings ~10-20% on top of TSM speedup. This is the canonical 'pause/silence trimming' player and the model others copy.

results.title: speech-speed: Chrome extension - dynamic content-aware playback rate
results.snippet: A working content-aware / variable-rate tool: dynamically adjusts playback speed based on how fast the speaker is currently talking, targeting a desired EFFECTIVE syllable rate (configurable 4-14 syl/s, default 9). It speeds up slow/filler segments more and dense segments less, holding perceived syllable rate roughly constant - exactly the 'increase throughput without raising peak syllable rate' technique. Useful as a hackable model for a custom player that exceeds Audible's 3.5x cap and lets the user dial in a target syll/sec rather than a blunt multiplier.

results.title: Silence compression - Wikipedia
results.snippet: Defines the underlying technique behind every Smart-Speed-style feature: trimming detects audio intervals below an amplitude threshold and removes them entirely. Good concise reference for the 'truncate silence' / threshold-based pause-removal building block that the user (or a custom Rubber Band/SoundTouch re-encoding pipeline) would apply before or alongside time-scale modification to gain extra throughput at zero comprehension cost.

results.title: Pauses inserted between words increase cortical tracking of the speech envelope (PLOS One)
results.snippet: Neural evidence for the gap-insertion benefit: adding pauses between words increases cortical tracking of the speech envelope, indicating better alignment between neural processing and the signal. Supports the buffer-drain rationale at the brain level - BUT it also flags the trade-off the user must weigh: long inter-word gaps inflate total duration and can fragment phrase structure, so gaps must be placed at boundaries and sized to net-positive throughput rather than sprinkled everywhere.

results.title: Hacker News thread: "Try 5-6x... blind people who listen so fast it sounds like noise"
results.snippet: The single best practitioner source for sub-Q1 (training) and Q5 (community methods). Contains the exact community ramping protocol the orchestrator described: listen one tier above comfort for ~10 min, drop back down, do as many 'reps' as possible, and after 3-4 books a new tier becomes your 'normal'; then 'bump the rate up another 0.25 and rinse and repeat' until 'it's incomprehensible to anyone but you.' Also gives realistic SIGHTED ceilings from enthusiasts: comfortable 1.75-2.5x, '2x+ without much effort,' max ~1.5x for expressive fiction vs 2-3x for non-fiction. Notes the real-life-impatience side effect of high-speed adaptation. Anchors why a sighted person near 3.5x is already at the upper practitioner band, making '7x/10x' physically implausible without trimming/throughput tricks.

results.title: Why Can Some Blind People Process Speech Far Faster Than Sighted Persons? (Scientific American)
results.snippet: Directly answers Q2 (the sighted ceiling vs trained-blind ceiling) in the exact syllables/second terms the orchestrator needs. States normal speech is ~6 syll/sec, the sighted comprehension limit is ~10 syll/sec (fast commercial-disclaimer territory), and trained blind listeners reach up to ~22-25 syll/sec. This is the citable evidence that there is NO documented case of sighted listeners reaching the 16-22 syll/sec range; it reframes the user's 3.5x (~13 syll/sec) as already AT or slightly ABOVE the typical sighted ceiling, and confirms 7x/10x (~26-37+ syll/sec) exceeds even the trained-blind maximum.

results.title: NVDA users group: "Tips for speed reading/listening with screen readers"
results.snippet: Direct Q5/Q4 practitioner source: a blind/NVDA power-user forum thread specifically on training to listen faster with a screen reader (could not be fetched live, HTTP 402, but the thread is the canonical community discussion). Relevant because screen-reader TTS is the main tool category that exceeds Audible's 3.5x cap, and synth voices (eSpeak NG in NVDA) remain intelligible at very high rates where natural-voice TSM artifacts dominate. The orchestrator should treat synthetic TTS as the practical path to high throughput, since blind power users routinely run eSpeak well past natural-narration speeds.

results.title: Pocket Casts Playback Effects (Trim Silence, up to 5x speed)
results.snippet: Concrete tool answer for Q4 and Q3. Documents a mainstream player exceeding Audible's 3.5x cap (speed up to 5x) AND the throughput-without-syllable-rate technique: Trim Silence removes pauses 'without altering the cadence of the host.' This is the key practitioner lever for the user's goal: combining content-aware silence trimming with speed multiplies effective WPM without raising syllable rate beyond the comprehension ceiling. Pairs with Overcast's Smart Speed (reported 2-3 hrs/week saved) as the silence-removal class of tools.

results.title: What determines the speed of speech recognition? Evidence from congenitally blind adults (Neuropsychologia)
results.snippet: Supports Q2/Q3 with the mechanism behind the ceiling. The large-scale listening-rate study (expanded from 453 to 1,409 participants, screen-reader-accessible, accuracy-at-rate methodology) underpins the throughput discussion: comprehension is gated by phonological processing/buffer drain, not just ear speed. Justifies the Q3 strategy of inserting silent gaps to drain the phonological-storage buffer and content-aware chunking, and explains why blind listeners' advantage is processing-based and not readily transferable to a sighted adult via simple practice.

results.title: How to Change Audible Speed on All Devices (and exceed the 3.5x cap via conversion)
results.snippet: Practical Q4 answer for getting PAST Audible's hard 3.5x limit: confirms Audible's range is 0.5x-3.5x and that the workaround is converting DRM'd Audible files to an open format (e.g. MP3) so any third-party player or re-encoder can apply arbitrary speed and silence-trimming. This is the bridge to custom players / ffmpeg atempo / Rubber Band / SoundTouch re-encoding pipelines the user wants, which standard Audible cannot do.

results.title: The perceptual learning of time-compressed speech: A comparison of training protocols with different levels of difficulty (Banai & Lavner, PLOS One 2017)
results.snippet: Directly answers the 'progressive ramp vs. constant-high overload' question. Compared 4 protocols in a ~1-hour session (5 blocks x 60 trials = 300 trials). Easy-start protocols (constant-low at 60% of natural duration, and adaptive-incremental 60%->50%->40%->30%->20%) significantly beat constant-high (30% throughout). The ramped group held .91 accuracy at the hardest 30% level vs .76 for those trained only at 30%, and generalized better to novel sentences/talkers. Practical takeaway: start near-natural speed and ramp difficulty gradually rather than overloading from the start. Natural baseline given as ~109 wpm.

results.title: Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation (Banai & Lavner, PLOS One 2012)
results.snippet: Establishes the two-phase learning model: an initial RAPID adaptation phase (identification improves within just 10-20 sentences, even at 38% of original duration) followed by a slower, more stimulus-specific learning phase requiring multi-day practice. Crucial for setting expectations on how long gains take: the easy wins come fast, but pushing the ceiling and getting durable, generalizable gains needs sustained multi-session training. Multiday training yields significantly more learning than brief adaptation alone.

results.title: The effects of training length on the perceptual learning of time-compressed speech and its generalization (Banai & Lavner, JASA 2014)
results.snippet: Quantifies the dose-response of training length. One vs. three training sessions vs. brief exposure vs. naive: all trained listeners beat controls on trained material, but ONLY the three-session group generalized to untrained tokens. Effect sizes vs. untrained controls were large (Cohen's d > 0.8) but learning was 'fairly specific.' Key practitioner implication: a single session boosts trained items; multiple sessions are needed before gains transfer to new content/voices — supports a multi-day ramp schedule for the user.

results.title: Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann, Front. Hum. Neurosci. 2013)
results.snippet: Directly addresses the sighted-vs-blind ceiling and what training can achieve. Over a ~6-month training period targeting 18 syll/sec, 4 of 6 late-blind participants reached ~70% words-correct; trained blind listeners reach up to ~22 syll/sec vs untrained sighted ceiling ~8 syll/sec. Critically, the gains were tied to cross-modal recruitment of primary visual cortex/fusiform/pulvinar — a mechanism unavailable to sighted listeners — suggesting the blind 16-22 syll/sec range is NOT a realistic sighted target. Establishes both the training timeline (months) and the physiological reason for the sighted limit.

results.title: Effects of stimulus repetition and training schedule on the perceptual learning of time-compressed speech and its transfer (Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics 2019)
results.snippet: Refines the practical schedule design: examines how stimulus repetition and the spacing/scheduling of training affect both learning magnitude and transfer to untrained materials. Useful for the 'how to structure daily practice and break plateaus' part of the question — informs whether to vary material (better transfer) vs. repeat (faster within-set gains).

results.title: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – an fMRI study (Hertrich et al., BMC Neuroscience 2013)
results.snippet: Provides the concrete syllable-rate benchmarks needed to translate the user's 'x' multipliers honestly: trained blind listeners comprehend 17-22 syll/sec (~680-880 wpm), versus a normal-sighted ceiling around 8 syll/sec. Documents that the blind advantage rests on visual-cortex recruitment correlated with syllable onsets, reinforcing that the very top end is mechanism-dependent and likely out of reach for a sighted listener — relevant for assessing whether '7x'/'10x' is physically achievable.

results.title: Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans (Dietrich, Hertrich, Ackermann, 2013)
results.snippet: Core ceiling paper. Establishes the documented numbers the feasibility assessment hinges on: blind 'experts' comprehend up to ~22 syllables/s; normal-sighted listeners cap at ~8 syl/s (Moos & Trouvain). Also documents a ~6-month training protocol: 8 late-blind participants improved sentence-repetition accuracy from 9% pre-training to 70% post-training at ultra-fast (18 syl/s) rates. Crucially, the dramatic 22 syl/s outcomes recruit cross-modal VISUAL cortex (recruited only after vision loss), implying the sighted ceiling is materially lower than the blind ceiling — directly grounding why the user's '10x' (~37 syl/s) is not physically achievable for a sighted person and '7x' (~26 syl/s) is above even the trained-blind max.

results.title: Comprehension of Ultra-Fast Speech — Blind vs. 'Normally Hearing' Persons (Moos & Trouvain, 2007)
results.snippet: The originating source for the canonical figures. Normal speaking rate is 4-8 syl/s; daily-practiced blind listeners comprehend synthetic speech up to ~22 syl/s while untrained sighted persons fail well below that. Compares natural speech, formant synthesis, and natural-speech-with/without-pauses, so it speaks directly to the 'throughput without raising syllable rate' question (pause handling) and fixes the honest sighted baseline against which the user's 3.5x (~13 syl/s) and target 7x/10x must be judged.

results.title: The perceptual learning of time-compressed speech: a comparison of training protocols with different levels of difficulty (Banai & Lavner)
results.snippet: The most directly actionable training paper for a SIGHTED user (65 normal-hearing adults). Compares constant-low (60% duration), constant-high (30%), adaptive-staircase (65%→20% via 2-down/1-up), and adaptive-incremental (60/50/40/30/20% across blocks). Finding: progressive/adaptive overload and constant-low both produce significant gains and BETTER generalization to novel material than constant-high 'too hard' practice — i.e., ramp gradually, don't max out. Baseline ~109 wpm natural; single ~1-hour sessions of 300 trials produced measurable learning. Supports the 'progressive ramp beats plateau-jumping' advice.

results.title: On the possible role of brain rhythms in speech perception: intelligibility of time-compressed speech with periodic and aperiodic insertions of silence (Ghitza & Greenberg, 2009)
results.snippet: The key 'throughput without raising syllable rate' / phonological-buffer-draining evidence. Speech compressed to 1/3 duration (3x, normally unintelligible, ~50% word-error) becomes intelligible again when silent gaps are inserted periodically. Word-error is U-shaped vs gap length: optimum at ~80 ms gaps inserted after 40 ms speech chunks (restoring ~120 ms / theta-rhythm packaging). Directly justifies 'compress hard then re-insert periodic ~80 ms silences / chunk to the syllabic-theta rhythm' as a way to raise information throughput without forcing the raw syllable rate higher.

results.title: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar — an fMRI study (Hertrich, Dietrich, Ackermann, BMC Neuroscience 2013)
results.snippet: Reinforces the mechanistic 'sighted vs blind' divide. Blind experts proficient at ~16-22 syl/s ultra-fast speech show right primary visual cortex + fusiform + pulvinar activation absent in sighted listeners. This cross-modal recruitment is the leading explanation for WHY blind listeners exceed the sighted ceiling — the honest takeaway being that the top of the blind range is not transferable to sighted learners, bounding realistic sighted gains below ~16 syl/s.

results.title: Tips for speed reading/listening with screen readers (NVDA community thread) + libsonic/eSpeak rate-boost notes
results.snippet: Practitioner/power-user knowledge from blind screen-reader users. Concrete adaptation protocol matching the academic ramp: raise speech rate ~5% per week until slightly challenging, then drop 5% and consolidate for weeks before climbing again. Notes tooling that exceeds typical player caps — NVDA's 'libsonic'/rate-boost effectively overclocks speech and newest eSpeak runs ~2x faster than JAWS top speed, plus 'skim first 2-3 words' chunking. Bridges the academic ceiling to real-world tools and the user's request for players beyond Audible's 3.5x cap.

claims.claim: Overcast's 'Smart Speed' increases information throughput by trimming/shortening silences and pauses in audio, rather than by raising the playback speed or syllable rate.
claims.quote: Smart Speed shortens silences.

claims.claim: Silence-trimming delivers time savings comparable to an additional playback-speed step but without the audible degradation that raising speed causes — i.e. it is an independent throughput lever from speed multipliers.
claims.quote: Smart Speed is like getting another speed increment for free: it saves time without sounding weird.

claims.claim: Conventional approach of raising playback speed degrades sound quality and intelligibility, which is the limitation silence-trimming is meant to avoid.
claims.quote: Playing at faster speeds has always helped people make time to hear more podcasts, but it usually came at the expense of sound quality and intelligibility.

claims.claim: Silence/pause trimming can be applied with no perceptible artifacts and can make speech sound more natural (tighter timing) rather than distorted, supporting its use as a non-degrading throughput technique.
claims.quote: Conversations still sounded natural — often better, with tighter timing.

claims.claim: The speech-speed Chrome extension implements content-aware variable-rate playback that normalizes speech to a constant target syllable rate (default 9 syllables/second) by speeding up slow speakers more and fast speakers less, rather than applying a single fixed multiplier.
claims.quote: The goal is to normalize all speech to a comfortable listening rate ... the default target of 9 syl/s. Slow speakers get sped up more; fast speakers get sped up less.

claims.claim: The extension detects natural syllable rate via energy-envelope modulation analysis, high-pass filtering RMS to isolate the 2-10 Hz syllable-rate band and counting positive-going zero-crossings, demonstrating a practical real-time method for the throughput technique of equalizing perceived rate without a global pitch/speed change.
claims.quote: High-pass filter the raw RMS to remove DC and slow-varying level, leaving only syllable-rate modulation (2-10 Hz).

claims.claim: The tool maps speaker pace to playback rate so that a very slow speaker (~2.5 syl/s) is sped up to 3.50x while a fast speaker (~8.0 syl/s) is sped to only 1.12x, providing concrete speaker-pace-to-multiplier data points that anchor 'x' multipliers to syllables/second.
claims.quote: ranging from 3.50x for very slow (~2.5 syl/s) down to 1.12x for very fast speakers (~8.0 syl/s)

claims.claim: The extension's default maximum playback ceiling is 3.5x, configurable up to 5x, so it does not natively exceed Audible's 3.5x cap by default and only modestly exceeds it at the upper bound, indicating this tool is not a high-multiplier (7x-10x) solution.
claims.quote: Max speed (1.5-5x, default 3.5): ceiling for playback rate. Protects against extreme speedup on very slow speakers.

claims.claim: Rather than trimming silences, the extension gradually drifts playback speed back toward 1x during silences (low energy or low syllable rate exceeding 3 seconds), a different throughput strategy than pause/silence removal or buffer-drain gap insertion.
claims.quote: During silence (low energy or low syllable rate for more than 3 seconds), the speed gradually drifts back toward 1x.

claims.claim: Silence trimming is a defined audio-processing technique in which silent intervals are detected and removed altogether from a recording, which directly underlies the 'pause/silence trimming' throughput method for shortening audio duration without altering speech-rate.
claims.quote: Trimming is a method of silence compression in which the silent intervals are removed altogether.

claims.claim: Silence trimming permanently alters the original audio and can introduce audible artifacts on playback, which is a real-world cost of aggressive pause-trimming for speed listening.
claims.quote: a drawback of trimming is that it permanently changes the original audio and can cause noticeable artifacts when the audio is played back.

claims.claim: Silence detection can be done via a fixed amplitude threshold, removing segments below it, but choosing the right threshold is hard because of varying recording conditions and sources.
claims.quote: Any audio segments that fall below this threshold are removed.

claims.claim: Energy-based silence detection (E equals the sum of squared sample amplitudes) detects silence more accurately than amplitude-based trimming, offering a more robust basis for automated pause-removal tools.
claims.quote: can detect silence more accurately than amplitude-based trimming.

claims.claim: The article provides no quantitative data on time savings, optimal silence thresholds, gap ratios, or comprehension effects, so it does not establish optimal silent-gap timing for draining the phonological buffer.
claims.quote: Silence compression is an audio processing technique used to effectively encode silent intervals, reducing the amount of storage or bandwidth needed to transmit audio recordings.

claims.claim: Inserting silent pauses at clause and sentence boundaries into time-compressed speech ('time-restoration') restored recall accuracy of normal-hearing young adults to a level statistically indistinguishable from normal-rate speech (~95% recovery, p=.874), even though the words were still delivered at the fast compressed syllable rate.
claims.quote: Experiment 1 was conducted with 27 normal-hearing young adults as a proof-of-concept demonstration that restoring lost processing time by inserting silent pauses at linguistically salient points within a time-compressed narrative ("time-restoration") returns recall accuracy to a level approximating that for a normal speech rate.

claims.claim: The optimal gap-insertion scheme placed silence only at linguistic boundaries (not uniformly), with sentence-boundary pauses set at twice the duration of clause-boundary pauses, and total inserted silence calibrated so the time-restored passage matched the duration of the original uncompressed recording.
claims.quote: the durations of the silent periods added at sentence boundaries were set at twice the duration of the silent periods added at clause boundaries ... the total duration of the silent periods was set such that the duration of the time-restored compressed passages was equal to the duration of the original un-compressed recordings

claims.claim: The deficit from fast speech is attributed to loss of cognitive processing time rather than perceptual intelligibility, and restoring that time via pauses reduced listening effort as measured by pupil dilation.
claims.quote: the rapidity of time-compressed speech can deprive the listener of the ordinarily available processing time present when speech is delivered at a normal speech rate ... Pupil dilation indicated that additional effort was expended by participants while attempting to process the time-compressed narratives, with the effortful demand on resources reduced with time restoration.

claims.claim: The speech was time-compressed to 60% of original duration, raising the rate from a normal mean of 173.4 wpm to 288.71 wpm (~1.67x), at which recall declined significantly before pause restoration recovered it.
claims.quote: time-compressed to be reproduced to 60% of their original playing time ... equivalent to a mean speech rate of 288.71 wpm

claims.claim: The benefit of pause-restoration depended on cognitive capacity: for cochlear-implant users, meaningful recovery of recall was limited to a subgroup with better working memory spans and word/sentence recognition scores.
claims.quote: meaningful recovery of recall accuracy with time-restoration was limited to a subgroup of CI users identified by better working memory spans, and better word and sentence recognition scores

claims.claim: Inserting 100ms silences between syllables of time-compressed speech (repackaging) to restore the syllabic rate to ~6.1 sps significantly improves intelligibility across all spectral conditions tested — a throughput technique that raises comprehension without lowering the underlying syllable production rate.
claims.quote: There was a significant increase in intelligibility when the speech rate was restored to 6.1 sps by adding silences and that for all of the spectral content conditions

claims.claim: Normal-hearing young adults' intelligibility collapses at high syllable rates: 99.8% at the control rate of 6.6 sps versus only 41.9% at 15.6 sps — quantifying the steep comprehension ceiling for untrained sighted listeners well below the 16-22 sps range trained blind listeners achieve.
claims.quote: the average intelligibility across listeners was significantly reduced to 41.9% (SD = 18.0%, range = 12.5–84.4%)

claims.claim: The optimal restoration gap was a fixed 100ms silence inserted after each syllable at zero-crossings, concatenated in original order, bringing the rate back to a natural processing window (~6.1 sps).
claims.quote: 100 ms of silence was added after each syllable and all silence-included syllables were concatenated in the same order as the original speech

claims.claim: Temporal fine structure (TFS) / spectral resolution governs how much benefit silence-insertion provides: repackaging gains grew with richer spectral content (4.97% at 8-channel vocoder, 16.46% at 16-channel, 23.40% with original spectral content), implying high-fidelity audio matters for fast-speech comprehension techniques.
claims.quote: The results show TFS contributes significantly to the perception of fast speech even when the overall rhythm/envelope of TC speech is restored

results.title: eSpeak + libsonic: high-speed TTS for trained speed-listeners (up to 700-1000 wpm)
results.snippet: This is the single most important tool path for exceeding the 3.5x cap. The 'sonic' library (waywardgeek/sonic on GitHub), integrated into eSpeak/eSpeak NG, was built explicitly so blind users can drive open-source TTS at 'ultra-fast' rates: eSpeak NG supports sonic for up to ~700 wpm without pitch shift, and speech-dispatcher can be configured to ~1000 wpm, with practitioners reporting comprehension at 500-800 wpm. Sonic is algorithmically optimized for >2x speedups (unlike WSOLA/PSOLA which degrade), which is why it is the de-facto throughput tool for trained ears. For a sighted user wanting '7x-10x', converting text/audiobooks to text and feeding them through eSpeak NG+sonic via a screen reader (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver) is the realistic high-rate path, far beyond any audiobook app's 4x ceiling. Note: 800 wpm against a ~150 wpm slow-narration baseline is ~5.3x; against ~13 syll/sec it implies the ceiling is near the trained-blind limit, not 10x.

results.title: sonic — library to speed up speech, built for the blind/VI to exceed 2x without artifacts
results.snippet: The actual re-encoding/playback engine behind high-rate screen-reader TTS. README states the explicit motivation: enable blind and visually impaired users to boost productivity with open-source speech engines like eSpeak, and that it is optimized for speedups 'over 2X' where older algorithms fail. This is the SoundTouch/Rubber Band alternative purpose-built for speech-at-speed; it can be linked into custom players or TTS pipelines, making it the key building block for a tool that supports both high syllable rates AND the pause-trimming the user asked about.

results.title: FFmpeg rubberband / atempo re-encoding for >2x audiobook speedup with pitch preserved
results.snippet: Practical re-encode workflow that bypasses Audible's 3.5x cap entirely: pre-process audiobooks to a fixed higher tempo and play the result anywhere. Confirms atempo only accepts 0.5-2.0 so 4x needs chaining (atempo=2.0,atempo=2.0) and that plain atempo causes chipmunk/artifact issues at 3x+, whereas the rubberband filter (librubberband) preserves pitch and quality. Directly relevant to the 'Rubber Band/SoundTouch re-encoding' angle, including the candid note that 'anything above 5x is barely useful' — useful for the honest 7x/10x feasibility assessment. Pairs with FFmpeg's silencedetect/silenceremove filters for the user's pause-trimming/throughput-without-raising-rate goal.

results.title: Rubber Band vs SoundTouch — time-stretch engine tradeoffs for high-rate speech
results.snippet: Directly compares the two re-encoding libraries the user named. Rubber Band works in the frequency domain for highest quality and handles transients well (less 'phasiness'), but is too CPU-heavy for real-time mobile use — so it suits offline re-encode workflows. SoundTouch is lighter (mobile-friendly, the engine many players use) but does not handle transients. Explains why offline FFmpeg+rubberband re-encoding gives cleaner high-speed output than a phone player's real-time SoundTouch, and frames the throughput discussion (mentions Overcast Smart Speed silence-trimming saving 10-15% without raising rate).

results.title: Smart AudioBook Player (Android) — variable speed + configurable skip-silence
results.snippet: Popular, accessibility-friendly real-world player supporting the throughput techniques the user wants short of full re-encoding: a 'Skip Silence' control with a configurable maximum-silence threshold (long-press exposes 0.1s-0.9s values) to trim pauses and drain dead air, plus pitch adjustment, equalizer, noise reduction and Opus conversion. Good example of a player whose silence-trimming + speed combine for higher effective throughput than Audible, though its raw speed ceiling is still modest (low single-digit x), so it complements rather than replaces the TTS/re-encode paths.

results.title: AudioGames.net forum — what speech rates blind/screen-reader power users actually run
results.snippet: Practitioner-knowledge thread (the requested community angle). Blind/screen-reader power users discuss the rates they sustain daily, with reports of listening around 800 wpm and references to NVDA using libsonic to push eSpeak above 100% ('overclocking' speech), plus notes that different voices (eSpeak vs others) behave differently at extreme rates (stutter/clicking). Grounds the theoretical trained-blind ceiling in lived practice and reinforces that ~800 wpm (~5x slow-narration) is the upper band real users operate at — useful evidence against a literal '10x' target for a sighted listener.

claims.claim: Inserting silent pauses between words (250 ms or 500 ms) significantly increases cortical/neural tracking of the speech envelope compared to natural speech, with reconstruction correlations rising progressively as pause length increases in both delta and theta bands.
claims.quote: continuous speech with additional pauses inserted between words significantly increases the speech envelope reconstruction correlations compared to using natural speech ... the correlation coefficients gradually increase across the speech pause conditions in both the delta and theta bands (p˃–0.001, Friedman tests)

claims.claim: The study tested specific inter-word pause durations of 250 ms (short) and 500 ms (long) inserted between every word, against a no-added-pause natural baseline, providing concrete timing parameters for inserting silent gaps between speech units.
claims.quote: Short pauses: 250 ms between each word (~16 minutes total) - Long pauses: 500 ms between each word (~21 minutes total)

claims.claim: Increased cortical tracking from inserted pauses does NOT directly establish improved comprehension/intelligibility; the relationship requires further investigation, so the buffer-drain/pause benefit for understanding is not confirmed by neural tracking alone.
claims.quote: Whether the increased cortical response...reflect improved intelligibility requires further investigation

claims.claim: Modifying only pause duration (without altering phoneme acoustics) isolates the effect of timing/gaps from acoustic time-compression effects, relevant to throughput techniques that add silent gaps without changing syllable rate.
claims.quote: only modifying the duration of pauses...its effect on speech intelligibility can be investigated independently from effects of changes in acoustical properties

claims.claim: The absolute comprehension limit for sighted listeners is approximately 10 syllables per second, exemplified by fast radio announcers reading fine print.
claims.quote: That hyperactive radio announcer spewing fine print at the end of a commercial jabbers at 10 syllables per second, the absolute limit of comprehension for sighted people.

claims.claim: Blind listeners can comprehend speech sped up to 25 syllables per second, far exceeding the sighted ceiling.
claims.quote: Blind people, however, can comprehend speech sped up to 25 syllables per second.

claims.claim: Normal rapid conversational speech occurs at about six syllables per second, providing a baseline against which compression multipliers can be measured.
claims.quote: When we speak rapidly we are verbalizing at about six syllables per second.

claims.claim: Speech at the 25 syllables/second rate cannot be produced by a human speaker and must be generated by a computerized synthesizer, making it a laboratory rather than naturally occurring finding.
claims.quote: had to use a computerized synthesizer to generate speech at this speed

claims.claim: The blind subjects in the study had lost their sight between two and 15 years of age, indicating the fast-processing ability was documented in early/childhood-onset blind individuals rather than congenitally blind from birth.
claims.quote: All the subjects in this study had lost their sight between two and 15 years of age.

claims.claim: Sighted speed-listening practitioners report a comfortable sustained range of roughly 1.5x-3x for general podcast/YouTube/audiobook content, with the ceiling being content-dependent (non-fiction tolerates faster than expressive fiction with accents, which can cap around 1.5x).
claims.quote: listen to podcasts, YouTube, etc at 1.5-3x ... good non-fiction reader...I can go faster versus fiction book...sometimes can't get beyond 1.5

claims.claim: Practitioners describe an adaptation/overload mechanism where listening speed can be progressively ramped because the brain habituates to any rate and then grows impatient, eventually reaching speeds incomprehensible to untrained listeners.
claims.quote: no matter how fast the speech rate is, you'll eventually get used to it and your brain will grow impatient

claims.claim: Sighted commenters report that blind listeners reach speeds far beyond their own, fast enough to sound like noise to an untrained sighted person, consistent with a large gap between trained-blind and untrained-sighted ceilings.
claims.quote: I've met some blind people who listen to audio so fast, it sounds like noise to me.

claims.claim: Even highly habituated sighted practitioners describe roughly 6x as a qualitatively different regime ('something else entirely'), implying the practical sighted comfort ceiling for these enthusiasts sits well below 6x rather than at it.
claims.quote: 6x is just something else entirely

claims.claim: High-speed listening produces a documented behavioral side effect: habituated listeners become impatient with normal-speed speech and real-life conversation.
claims.quote: I've found myself grow impatient listening to people in real life and can trace it back to high-speed listening habits

claims.claim: Pocket Casts web/desktop apps support playback speed up to 5x, exceeding Audible's 3.5x cap, while mobile apps cap at 3x.
claims.quote: Mobile Apps (iOS/Android): Range from 0.5x to 3x speed; Web/Desktop Apps: Range from 0.5x to 5x speed

claims.claim: Pocket Casts offers a Trim Silence feature with three selectable intensity levels (Mild, Medium, Mad Max) that removes silences without altering the speaker's cadence or pitch, increasing throughput independently of syllable rate.
claims.quote: Trim silences from audio without altering the cadence of the host (sometimes called 'smart silence' or 'silence removal'). Trim Silence works with both online and local files.

claims.claim: Pocket Casts includes a Volume Boost toggle that raises playback volume without changing device volume, supporting intelligibility at high speeds.
claims.quote: Increases the volume of playback without changing your device volume.

claims.claim: Pocket Casts playback effects (Trim Silence, Volume Boost) are only available on mobile apps; web and desktop apps support only the playback speed change (which goes up to 5x).
claims.quote: Android and iOS mobile apps support all three effects; Web and desktop apps only support playback speed changes

claims.claim: Audible's native playback speed is capped at a maximum of 3.5x (range 0.5x to 3.5x via slider) on both iOS and the desktop Cloud Player.
claims.quote: Choose your desired speed from the options (0.7x-2.0x), or use the slider to fine-tune the Audible reading speed from 0.5x to 3.5x.

claims.claim: To exceed Audible's native speed cap, the recommended workaround is to convert Audible audiobooks into an open format (MP3) and play them in a third-party 'professional audiobook player' that supports higher speeds.
claims.quote: If none of the fixes above work and you want a long-term solution, try playing your audiobook in a professional audiobook player. To do that, you first need to use ViWizard Audible Converter to turn your Audible book into an open format like MP3.

claims.claim: Converting Audible books to open formats (MP3, FLAC, M4A, M4B, WAV, AIFF) enables playback on any third-party player or device, including players such as VLC, WorkAudioBook, Smart Audiobook Player, Listen Audiobook Player, BookPlayer, and Bound.
claims.quote: Convert Audible books to MP3, FLAC, M4A, M4B, WAV, or AIFF so you can play them on any player or device offline.

claims.claim: Perceptual learning of time-compressed speech progresses in two phases: an initial rapid/general phase followed by a prolonged, more stimulus-specific phase — meaning gains require multiple sessions rather than a single sitting, with most improvement occurring between sessions 1 and 2.
claims.quote: learning of time-compressed speech progresses through an initial general phase and a subsequent prolonged and more stimulus-specific phase

claims.claim: A structured training protocol of 5 sessions over 10 days (300 sentences per session) using an adaptive two-down one-up staircase produced large, measurable comprehension gains (Cohen's d = 1.37), establishing a concrete evidence-based ramp/overload schedule for improving time-compressed speech comprehension.
claims.quote: Five sessions were administered over the course of 10 days. During each session, listeners had to verify sentences taken from the training set

claims.claim: Training generalized to new speakers (both male and female) presenting the trained sentences, but did NOT transfer from sentence-level to word-level (single word/lexical decision) tasks — indicating learned gains are partly stimulus-specific and do not universally transfer across all material types.
claims.quote: trained listeners significantly generalized their learning to the two untrained conditions in which different speakers presented the trained sentences

claims.claim: Adaptive training was anchored at a 65% initial compression level with the staircase ranging 20-65% of original duration, against source material whose original rate averaged only 109 words/minute — a low baseline rate relevant to translating speed multipliers into words-per-minute.
claims.quote: A two-down one-up procedure was employed, with an initial compression level of 65%.

claims.claim: Trained listeners' performance plateaued at the level of native speakers on trained conditions but remained below native performance on untrained sentence conditions, indicating a practical ceiling where learning does not fully generalize beyond practiced material.
claims.quote: performance of the trained group was equivalent to that of the native speakers on all conditions on which learning occurred

claims.claim: The documented upper limit of comprehension for normal-sighted listeners is approximately 8 syllables per second, far below blind listeners' ceiling.
claims.quote: normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s

claims.claim: Trained late-blind individuals can comprehend accelerated speech at rates up to approximately 22 syllables per second.
claims.quote: vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)

claims.claim: After approximately 6 months of training, four of six late-blind participants improved from below 14% to about 70% comprehension accuracy at 18 syllables per second.
claims.quote: four subjects (nos. 147, 150, 144, and 142) showed an improved behavioral performance of up to ca. 70% points

claims.claim: The training protocol involved at least 1 hour of daily practice over about 6 months using the JAWS screen reader, progressively increasing playback speed through intermediate comprehension milestones (80% at 13 syl/s) toward a final target of 18 syl/s.
claims.quote: for at least 1 h per day

claims.claim: Training-induced recruitment of the central-visual system (right V1 and pulvinar) for ultra-fast speech occurred only in participants with the lowest residual vision, indicating sighted listeners lack this neural mechanism for exceeding the ~8 syl/s ceiling.
claims.quote: Only subjects with very low visual acuity displayed training-induced hemodynamic activation of the central-visual system.

claims.claim: Normal speech is produced at approximately 6 syllables per second, while blind adults can comprehend ultra-fast accelerated text-to-speech at 16 or more syllables per second — establishing the trained-blind ceiling far above normal rates.
claims.quote: Blind adults can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for "reading" texts at ultra-fast speaking rates with 16 syllables (or more) produced per second, which exceeds by far normal speech rates of approximately six syllables per second.

claims.claim: The speech-speed advantage of blind listeners is NOT due to faster low-level phonological encoding or lexical matching: blind and sighted adults are equally fast at implicit phonological encoding and lexical matching, as shown by ERP timing not differing between groups.
claims.quote: Together these results suggest that blind and sighted listeners are equally fast in implicit phonological encoding and lexical matching mechanisms... timing of Event Related Potential (ERP) results did not differ between blind and sighted participants.

claims.claim: The blind speed advantage arises from not needing to wait for visual-domain integration during lexical selection, whereas sighted listeners mandatorily run auditory-visual integration in speech recognition — implying the advantage is rooted in cross-modal processing architecture rather than a trainable auditory skill.
claims.quote: we speculate that lexical selection processes in blind adults do not need to wait for information from the visual domain, while auditory-visual integration mechanisms are mandatorily implemented in speech recognition routines of sighted adults.

claims.claim: Blind adults make faster lexical decision responses than sighted adults, but this speed-up is not restricted to phonologically overlapping (priming) trials, indicating a general rather than phonology-specific processing advantage.
claims.quote: Blind participants made faster lexical decision responses than sighted participants, yet their speeded responses were not restricted to phonologically overlapping trials.

claims.claim: It remains scientifically debated whether and how improved auditory discrimination enables speeded speech comprehension in congenitally blind adults — i.e., the mechanism behind the blind speed advantage is not settled.
claims.quote: It is a matter of debate, whether and how improved auditory discrimination abilities enable speeded speech comprehension in congenitally blind adults.

claims.claim: Training on time-compressed speech that begins with mild distortion (a progressive ramp from easy to hard) produces better learning than training that presents severe compression from the start; the constant-high (30% duration) group performed worst of all trained groups, while an adaptive-incremental ramp reached 91% word accuracy at the same 30% compression level where the constant-high group reached only 76%.
claims.quote: Training procedures that started off with relatively little signal distortion were advantageous compared to...severe distortions presented from the very beginning. ... adaptive-incremental achieved greater accuracy (M = .91) versus constant-high (M = .76).

claims.claim: Measurable perceptual-learning gains for time-compressed speech occur within a single ~1-hour session of training (5 blocks of 60 trials, 300 total), after which trained listeners identify compressed speech significantly more accurately than minimally-exposed controls.
claims.quote: The experiment was administered in one session of approximately one hour. ... After training, recognition of the trained materials was significantly more accurate in trained listeners than in listeners who received only brief exposure to time-compressed speech.

claims.claim: Adaptive/progressive training protocols generalize better to novel tokens, talkers, and rates than constant-rate training, meaning the choice of difficulty schedule affects transfer, not just trained-item performance.
claims.quote: Adaptive protocols elicited more generalization than...constant training protocols

claims.claim: The compression levels used in successful training correspond to speed multipliers of roughly 1.67x (60% duration), 3.33x (30% duration), and 5x (20% duration) relative to natural speech, with intelligibility severely impeded at the 30% (3.33x) and faster end before training.
claims.quote: Adaptive-staircase ranged from 65% of the naturally-spoken duration to maximal compression of 20%. ... 30% (difficult/severely impeded intelligibility)

claims.claim: Participants were normal-hearing sighted adults (university undergraduates aged 18-35), so the documented gains apply directly to a sighted listener rather than to trained blind listeners.
claims.quote: Sixty five native Hebrew speakers (ages 18–35, all undergraduate university students) with normal hearing and no history of language, learning or attention problems.

claims.claim: Brief exposure to time-compressed speech (just 20 sentences) produces measurable perceptual learning, but this rapid learning only improves comprehension of the specific trained material and does NOT generalize to untrained speech tokens.
claims.quote: Brief exposure to time-compressed speech yields both learning and generalization... Trained listeners outperformed the other groups of listeners on the trained condition, but only the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens.

claims.claim: Generalization of speed-listening skill to NEW, untrained material requires multi-session (three sessions) training; a single session is insufficient to produce transferable gains, indicating a progressive/longer training protocol is needed for real-world improvement.
claims.quote: only the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens. These findings suggest that although learning of distorted speech can occur rapidly, more stable learning and generalization might be achieved with longer, multi-session practice.

claims.claim: Learning of time-compressed (distorted) speech occurs rapidly at first, implying early measurable gains, but stable durable improvement is achieved only through prolonged, distributed multi-session practice.
claims.quote: although learning of distorted speech can occur rapidly, more stable learning and generalization might be achieved with longer, multi-session practice

claims.claim: The perceptual learning of time-compressed speech is consistent with the Reverse Hierarchy Theory, implying distinct mechanisms for fast initial exposure-based gains versus slow generalizable expertise — relevant to plateau-breaking and adaptation strategy.
claims.quote: the findings are consistent with the framework proposed by the Reverse Hierarchy Theory of perceptual learning

claims.claim: Trained late-blind listeners can comprehend accelerated (time-compressed) speech at up to approximately 22 syllables per second, whereas normal sighted listeners' upper limit is about 8 syllables per second.
claims.quote: vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s

claims.claim: The single normal-sighted control subject in the study reached a personal ceiling and reported that further training would not improve her ultra-fast speech perception, indicating a hard sighted limit well below the trained-blind range.
claims.quote: The sighted control subject of the present study reported that she had 'reached her limits' and that she feels additional training would not yield further improvements of ultra-fast speech perception.

claims.claim: The training protocol that produced ultra-fast comprehension was daily use of the JAWS screen-reader (Eloquence synthesizer) for at least one hour per day over roughly six months, with recording sessions about three months apart.
claims.quote: participants were instructed to use the screen-reader JAWS (male voice, synthesizer 'Eloquence,' [link]) for at least 1 h per day

claims.claim: Training-induced recruitment of primary visual cortex (V1) underlying ultra-fast speech comprehension occurred only in blind subjects with no or low residual vision, providing a neural mechanism (cross-modal plasticity) that sighted listeners lack and that explains why sighted ceilings are lower.
claims.quote: training-induced enhancement of primary visual activation occurs only in subjects with no or low residual vision

claims.claim: Speech time-compressed by 3x (syllable rhythm 3x faster than original) has poor intelligibility, scoring below 50% words correct, but inserting silent gaps restores intelligibility.
claims.quote: The TTS waveform was time-compressed by a factor of 3, creating a signal with a syllable rhythm three times faster than the original, and whose intelligibility is poor (<50% words correct).

claims.claim: Inserting silence into 3x-compressed speech yields a U-shaped word-error-rate curve, with the lowest error rate (highest intelligibility) achieved when silent gaps of 80 ms are inserted PERIODICALLY; the optimal silence duration is 80 ms.
claims.quote: The performance curve (word error rate as a function of mean duration of silence) was U-shaped. The lowest word error rate (i.e., highest intelligibility) occurred when the silence was 80 ms long and inserted periodically.

claims.claim: Periodic (regularly-timed) insertion of silent gaps is superior to aperiodic (random) insertion for recovering intelligibility of compressed speech; aperiodic insertion increases word error rate.
claims.quote: word error rate increased when the silence was inserted aperiodically.

claims.claim: Optimal intelligibility of compressed speech occurs when the effective syllable rhythm is brought back into the high theta-frequency brain-rhythm range (6-12 Hz), supporting a neural-oscillation chunking mechanism (TEMPO model) as the constraint on how fast speech can be decoded.
claims.quote: optimum intelligibility is achieved when the syllable rhythm is within the range of the high theta-frequency brain rhythms (6-12 Hz)

claims.claim: eSpeak combined with the sonic (libsonic) time-stretching library can produce intelligible sped-up speech above 3.2x the default rate, and eSpeak's speed setting was raised to allow up to 750 wpm (with speech-dispatcher configured up to 1000 wpm).
claims.quote: I can mostly understand it at over 3.2X speed up using sonic to speed up the output ... You can now set the eSpeak speed up to 750 ... I set speech-dispatcher to allow up to 1000 words per minute, which is too fast probably for anyone, but I can see people listening at 800.

claims.claim: Real blind/screen-reader power users were documented listening to TTS at very high word rates, with one user (Storm Dragon) at ~600 wpm and developers anticipating users reaching ~800 wpm.
claims.quote: Storm Dragon is listening at about 600 wpm now ... 800 wpm is high enough that we wont have users complaining ... it would not surprise me to see some of them going past 800 wpm down the road.

claims.claim: Comprehension at high TTS speeds is a trainable skill that improves with exposure over time, even when the speech is simply played faster without acoustic processing.
claims.quote: The evidence I've seen so far is that with training, speed listeners can achieve higher listening rates with simple sped up speech ... I suspect his speed will increase as he learns to listen to espeak at higher rates over time.

claims.claim: libsonic uses a simple time-domain speed-up algorithm optimized to work well above 2x without altering pitch/loudness, providing a tool path beyond consumer players for re-encoding speech faster.
claims.quote: Sonic just speeds up speech, without changing the relative loudness or duration of any part of it ... a very simple algorithm to speed up speech, similar to some other time-domain speed-up algorithms ... optimised to work well above 2X speed up

claims.claim: Even experienced developers found the extreme listening speeds of trained blind users hard to comprehend, implicitly contrasting trained-blind capability against typical (sighted) developer perception.
claims.quote: Frankly, I don't see how some of these blind guys are able to listen so fast, but it would not surprise me to see some of them going past 800 wpm down the road.

claims.claim: FFmpeg's rubberband filter changes audio tempo while preserving the original pitch, making it suitable for sped-up speech/lectures where intelligibility matters (rubberband=tempo=2.0 doubles tempo without pitch shift).
claims.quote: to **change tempo while preserving pitch**, use the `rubberband` filter ... `rubberband=tempo=2.0` doubles tempo while keeping the original pitch. Ideal for interviews, lectures, and anything where intelligibility matters.

claims.claim: A single FFmpeg atempo instance accepts a tempo range of 0.5x to 100x on FFmpeg 4.2+ (older versions are limited to 0.5x-2.0x), allowing single-pass speedups far beyond Audible's 3.5x cap.
claims.quote: a single `atempo` instance accepts 0.5–100×" on FFmpeg 4.2+, with older versions limited to 0.5–2.0

claims.claim: atempo filters can be chained to multiply the speedup factor without an upper limit (e.g. atempo=2.0,atempo=2.0,atempo=2.0 yields 8x), enabling arbitrarily high audio re-encode speeds.
claims.quote: **Chained atempo**: unlimited via multiplication (e.g., `atempo=2.0,atempo=2.0,atempo=2.0` = 8×)

claims.claim: Plain atempo without pitch correction produces a pitch artifact ('chipmunk' effect) at high speeds such as 3x, which is why rubberband is preferred for high-rate listening.
claims.quote: Plain `atempo` resamples audio, which makes 3× speech sound like a chipmunk.

claims.claim: Rubber Band (frequency-domain) preserves audio quality and handles transients well, making it higher-fidelity than time-domain alternatives for time-stretching.
claims.quote: The audio quality is great, as the author did a solid job researching audio algorithms... It handles audio transients well (not losing them) and does a great job in preserving audio quality

claims.claim: Rubber Band's CPU cost is too high for real-time processing on mobile devices, whereas SoundTouch is lightweight enough to run in real time on mobile.
claims.quote: Rubber Band's CPU load is so high that you cannot run it on a mobile device for real-time processing

claims.claim: SoundTouch's time-domain (overlapping-window) approach avoids phasiness but produces a 'compressed'-sounding result and mishandles transients by missing or doubling parts of the audio.
claims.quote: Time-domain stretching works with overlapping windows, providing absolutely no 'phasiness' with the cost of missing or doubling some parts of the audio... The overall result sounds too 'compressed'

claims.claim: SoundTouch is a compact library with acceptable CPU load that can run real-time on a mobile device, making it suitable for low-resource custom player implementations.
claims.quote: A nice, compact library with an acceptable CPU load, it can work real-time on a mobile device.

claims.claim: Sonic is a speech rate-changing algorithm specifically optimized for speedups over 2X, distinguishing it from prior speech-rate-change algorithms that were not optimized for that range.
claims.quote: Sonic is a simple algorithm for speeding up or slowing down speech. However, it's optimized for speed ups of over 2X, unlike previous algorithms for changing speech rate.

claims.claim: Sonic's primary design motivation was to let blind and visually-impaired users boost productivity with open-source speech engines like espeak, but it explicitly also serves sighted users (e.g., audiobook listening on Android), confirming the high-speed technique is not blind-specific.
claims.quote: The primary motivation behind Sonic is to enable the blind and visually impaired to improve their productivity with open source speech engines, like espeak. Sonic can also be used by the sighted. For example, Sonic can improve the experience of listening to an audio book on an Android phone.

claims.claim: Sonic uses a 12-point sinc FIR filter for interpolation in its pitch-change algorithm (replacing linear interpolation), which significantly reduces noise — most noticeably in low-sample-rate streams such as the 11,025 Hz output of the Eloquence TTS engine.
claims.quote: I upgraded the pitch change algorithm to use a 12-point sinc FIR filter for interpolation, rather than linearly interpolating between points. This significantly reduces noise introduced by the pitch change algorithm. It is most noticable in low-sample-rate streams, such as the 11,025 Hz output of the Eloquence TTS engine.

claims.claim: Sonic is a lightweight ANSI C library (with a native Java port) designed to integrate into streaming voice applications and TTS back ends, making it available as an embeddable component for custom high-speed players/screen-reader pipelines rather than a fixed-cap consumer app.
claims.quote: The Sonic library is a very simple ANSI C library that is designed to easily be integrated into streaming voice applications, like TTS back ends.

claims.claim: Sonic provides no documented upper speed limit and gives no figures in words-per-minute or syllables-per-second, nor any claims about human comprehension ceilings or training — its only stated quantitative target is the '>2X' optimization threshold.
claims.quote: it's optimized for speed ups of over 2X, unlike previous algorithms for changing speech rate

claims.claim: Sighted listeners without synthesis training comprehended compressed speech only up to the 9-14 syllables/second range, with comprehension declining continuously across that range; this is the practical sighted ceiling and corresponds to the most extreme rates of human speech production. This maps directly to the user's question: 3.5x of ~150 wpm slow narration is ~13 syll/sec, sitting at the very top of the documented untrained-sighted band, so the requested '7x' (~26 syll/sec) and '10x' (~37 syll/sec) lie far beyond any documented human comprehension and are not physically achievable.
claims.quote: These texts were presented to sighted listeners at rates between 9-14 s/s and to blind listeners between 17-22 s/s.

claims.claim: Trained late-blind/daily-synthesis-user listeners reached the upper ceiling: some blind subjects still understood formant-synthesized speech at 22 syllables/second, confirming ~22 s/s as the documented upper bound for trained listeners and that this skill is tied to years of daily training, not available to untrained sighted listeners.
claims.quote: The fact that some of the blind still understood speech at 22 s/s reveals the enormous flexibility of the brain in speech perception during the processing of ultra-fast speech.

claims.claim: The blind listeners' extreme advantage existed only for formant-synthesized speech, NOT for compressed natural speech; for natural speech the blind scored only slightly better than sighted, and the ability to comprehend ultra-fast synthetic speech did not transfer to ultra-fast compressed natural speech. This means the 22 s/s ceiling is achieved with hyper-articulated synthetic TTS, not with compressed audiobook narration — relevant to the user's tool choice (screen-reader TTS vs. re-encoded Audible audio).
claims.quote: the extra-ordinary listening skills found for formant synthetic speech is not found for natural speech in very fast modes

claims.claim: Removing silent and breath pauses from compressed natural speech produced almost no comprehension benefit, reaching statistical significance only at the highest sighted rates (13 and 14 s/s); comprehension tracked articulation rate (excluding pauses) more strongly than speaking rate (including pauses). This bears directly on the user's throughput-without-raising-syllable-rate question: pause/silence trimming only helps measurably once you are already near the rate ceiling, and the gain is small.
claims.quote: The deletion of silent and breath intervals does hardly affect comprehension... the level of understanding is more strongly correlated with articulation rate (excluding pauses) rather than with speaking rate (including pauses).

claims.claim: Training to ultra-fast comprehension reaches a plateau quickly for short-horizon adaptation — about 10 minutes for compressed natural speech and about 5 days for synthesised sentences — while the longitudinal effect of years of training (the blind subjects averaged 7 years of daily synthesis use) is largely unexplored. This is directly relevant to the user's plateau-breaking and time-to-gains question: rapid initial adaptation, but the large ceiling gains seen in blind listeners came from years of daily exposure.
claims.quote: Training effect reaches a plateau after 10 minutes for compressed natural speech [10] or after 5 days for synthesised sentences [6]. The longitudinal effect of years of training to ultra-fast speech is largely unexplored.

result.evidence: The claim is directly supported, near-verbatim, by the quoted text in the primary peer-reviewed source: Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann (2013), Frontiers in Human Neuroscience (PMC3805979), which states blind individuals "may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second...exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s." The 22-vs-8 pairing originates from Moos & Trouvain (2007), a real cited peer-reviewed ICPhS study, not a misread or marketing source. Independently corroborated: the same group's fMRI study (PMC3847124 / BMC Neuroscience 2013) reproduces the framing, and Scientific American reports 25 syl/s (blind) vs ~10 syl/s max (sighted) — same order of magnitude, not contradicting. No credible source disputes the directional finding. Minor qualifications that do NOT refute: (1) the 22 syl/s figure is cited from Moos & Trouvain rather than the authors' own measurement (their own training study reached ~18 syl/s at ~70% comprehension); (2) "8 syl/s" is the top of a stated 4-8 syl/s sighted range, with other sources putting the practical sighted ceiling near 10 syl/s. The claim accurately reports the source and the central comparison holds across multiple independent peer-reviewed studies.
result.counterSource: No genuine refutation found. Closest divergence: Scientific American (reporting the same research) gives ~10 syl/s as the sighted max and 25 syl/s for blind — slightly higher than the claim's figures but supporting the same conclusion, not contradicting it.

result.evidence: Claim is a faithful, near-verbatim paraphrase of the supporting quote, which appears EXACTLY in the primary source: Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann (2013), "Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans," Frontiers in Human Neuroscience (PMC3805979). The figures (22 syl/s trained blind; ~8 syl/s sighted upper limit) trace to a real peer-reviewed phonetics paper, Moos & Trouvain (2007), Proc. 16th ICPhS, Saarbrücken — a legitimate primary source, not marketing. The blind/sighted gap is empirically corroborated by a separate fMRI study from the same group (PMC3847124 / BMC Neuroscience 2013), which found blind proficient listeners reached >90% comprehension at 16 syl/s while sighted subjects fell consistently below 20%. The numbers are reproduced consistently across at least 3-4 independent papers, so they are not cherry-picked. The 2007/2013 dates are old but this is a stable physiological finding about human auditory comprehension ceilings, not a fast-moving tech field. No contradicting source was found. Minor qualification: the research question's framing of "11-16 syll/sec untrained sighted" is higher than this claim's 8 syl/s, because "8 syl/s" is Moos/Trouvain's specific operationalization of the sighted comprehension ceiling; this does not refute the claim, which accurately reports its source. Source quality (peer-reviewed neuroscience + phonetics) matches the claim's strength.

The supporting quote is genuine — PMC3805979 (Dietrich et al.) states verbatim that "The sighted control subject of the present study reported that she had 'reached her limits' and that she feels additional training would not yield further improvements of ultra-fast speech perception." So Part 1 of the claim (she self-reported a personal ceiling) is accurate.

But the claim's load-bearing conclusion — "indicating a HARD SIGHTED LIMIT WELL BELOW the trained-blind range" — is an overreach contradicted by the source itself on four points:

1. n=1 SUBJECTIVE SELF-REPORT, not a measured ceiling. There was exactly one sighted subject (#142). A single person's feeling that she "can't improve" is not evidence of a population-level biological limit, and the authors treat it cautiously, not as proof of a fixed ceiling.

2. "WELL BELOW" IS FALSE FOR THE MATCHED COMPARISON. In THIS study the sighted subject reached 18 syl/s at ~67% correct, versus ~70% for four blind subjects at the SAME 18 syl/s. That is roughly equal (≈3 points), NOT "well below." The 22 syl/s "trained-blind range" comes from PRIOR literature on other individuals, not a head-to-head comparison in this experiment.

3. "HARD LIMIT" IS CONTRADICTED BY THE PAPER. The authors explicitly suggest her plateau may reflect insufficient training duration, noting "a further extension of the training period... might have been beneficial," i.e., they attribute the blind/sighted gap to cross-modal visual-cortex recruitment and neuroplasticity (learned reorganization), NOT an innate biological ceiling.

4. The paper's own generic "sighted upper limit ~8 syl/s" statement is contradicted by its own subject reaching 18 syl/s, undermining any clean "hard ceiling" framing.

Source quality is high (primary peer-reviewed PMC article), but the claim mischaracterizes it: an anecdotal n=1 self-report is inflated into a "hard limit," and the gap is overstated since her measured performance nearly matched the blind subjects at the tested rate. Refuted as an overreach/misread of the cited source.

Verbatim finding: "The performance curve (word error rate as a function of mean duration of silence) was U-shaped. The lowest word error rate (i.e., highest intelligibility) occurred when the silence was 80 ms long and inserted periodically." No contradicting source found; the finding is the foundational empirical result behind Ghitza's TEMPO/theta-oscillation framework and is repeatedly cited/replicated (e.g., JASA 2018 aging study, Frontiers 2014). Source quality (peer-reviewed primary study) matches claim strength. One caveat that does NOT refute the claim: the 80-ms optimum is specific to the 40-ms-fragment + 3x paradigm and to theta-band re-synchronization, so it is not a universal "insert 80 ms anywhere" recipe -- but the claim as worded ("lowest error when 80 ms gaps inserted periodically into 3x-compressed speech") is precisely what the study reports.

The claim OVERREACHES and MISREADS its supporting quote. The quote ("the extra-ordinary listening skills found for formant synthetic speech is not found for natural speech in very fast modes," Moos & Trouvain 2007) refers only to a RELATIVE degradation — synthetic is somewhat MORE intelligible than natural at high rates — NOT to the blind advantage vanishing. The companion primary source (Moos, Hertrich, Dietrich, Trouvain, Ackermann 2008, amlap.org/~trouvain/Moos_et_al_2008.pdf) directly CONTRADICTS the strong claim. Its key finding: the blind subject was "capable of understanding ultra-fast natural AND synthetic speech" and "these effects did not emerge in any of the normal-sighted subjects." Tested at 16 syll/sec, the blind listener understood ultra-fast NATURAL speech that all six sighted controls could NOT understand. The synthetic-vs-natural gap is only a mild tendency: "recognition rate for ultra-fast speech TENDED to be higher in the case of synthetic as compared to accelerated natural stimuli." So the blind advantage for natural speech is LARGE, not "only slightly better than sighted," and it DID transfer to natural speech — refuting the claim's wording that the ability "did not transfer to ultra-fast compressed natural speech." DIRECTIONAL KERNEL IS PARTLY TRUE: formant/synthetic speech is more robust than linearly-compressed natural speech at extreme rates (corroborated across sources; Oliveira et al. 2016, isca-archive.org/speechprosody_2016/oliveirajr16, even found sighted OUTPERFORMED blind for natural compressed intelligibility at 9-19 syll/sec). PubMed 19390234 = Ghitza & Greenberg (2009), "On the possible role of brain rhythms in speech perception," Phonetica 66(1-2):113-26. The abstract states exactly: "The TTS waveform was time-compressed by a factor of 3, creating a signal with a syllable rhythm three times faster than the original, and whose intelligibility is poor (<50% words correct)." It further reports that inserting periodic silent intervals restored intelligibility (best around 80 ms gaps; restoration robust for gaps ~20-120 ms), matching the second half of the claim. CHECKLIST: (1) No overreach — both clauses map to the source. (2) No contradicting evidence found; the effect has been independently replicated (Ghitza 2012/2014; Bosker & Ghitza 2018; Penn et al. 2018; Ramus et al. 2021) and confirmed in a 2023 Scientific Reports paper. Recent work refines the MECHANISM (silence inserted at syllabic boundaries helps more than at fixed time points; raw delivery rate alone does not explain it) but does NOT dispute the empirical phenomenon the claim describes. (3) Peer-reviewed primary source + multiple replications — adequate for a non-extraordinary empirical claim. (4) Not outdated — stable psychoacoustics finding reconfirmed through 2023. (5) Not marketing/forum speculation. CAVEATS for synthesis (do not refute): the "<50%" figure is for synthetic TTS speech in a controlled words-correct task (natural-speech replication exists via Bosker & Ghitza 2018), and this is lab intelligibility, not equivalent to the user's real-world Audible 3.5x comprehension; "factor 3" here is relative to a normal-rate baseline, not Audible's slow-narration baseline.
result.counterSource: No credible counter-source found. Closest related work (Nature Sci Rep 2023, s41598-023-29755-x; Bosker & Ghitza 2018) replicates the effect and only refines the theoretical mechanism (syllabic-boundary timing vs. raw rate), which does not contradict the empirical claim.

CONTRADICTION CHECK: Ghitza (2014, PMC4082093) Experiment I reports ~96% accuracy at compression factor 3, which superficially conflicts with "<50%." BUT this is a STIMULUS-CONSTRUCTION difference, not a refutation: the 2009 study used TTS material chopped into uniform 40-ms fragments, whereas 2014 Exp I used naturally-segmented uncompressed speech preserving natural syllabic packaging. Crucially, BOTH papers explicitly confirm the load-bearing part of the claim — that inserting silent gaps ("repackaging") substantially RESTORES intelligibility of severely compressed speech (2014: "intelligibility was substantially restored when the information stream was re-packaged by inserting gaps"; optimal packaging rate ~9 Hz / theta band). QUALIFICATION for synthesis: the exact "<50% words correct at factor 3" number is condition-specific (TTS + 40-ms uniform fragments) and another careful study got 96% at factor 3 under different segmentation. The robust, transferable takeaway for the user is the gap-insertion/repackaging mechanism itself: optimal ~80 ms periodic silent gaps restoring a ~9 Hz (high-theta) syllabic packaging rate improves comprehension of heavily time-compressed speech. The directional claim (raw 3x compression hurts intelligibility; inserted silent gaps restore it) is well-supported.
