result.evidence: The claim is well-supported by the primary source AND independently corroborated, so it survives refutation. (1) PRIMARY SOURCE: Moos & Trouvain (2007), ICPhS proceedings — a legitimate peer-reviewed conference paper from established speech scientists. It tested blind listeners at 17-22 syll/sec vs sighted at 9-14 syll/sec and found formant synthesis specifically advantaged at high rates; the supporting quote ('the extra-ordinary listening skills found for formant synthetic speech is not found for natural speech in very fast modes') is consistent with the study's design and conclusion. (2) INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION #1 — the 2013 Frontiers/PMC training+fMRI study (PMC3805979) used EXCLUSIVELY formant-synthesized JAWS 'Eloquence' TTS for all training and reached only ~70% comprehension at 18 syll/sec; it provides no evidence that trained blind listeners hit ~22 syll/sec with natural compressed speech. This confirms the documented extreme rates are a synthetic-TTS phenomenon, not compressed-audiobook narration. (3) INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION #2 — multiple sources confirm blind listeners specifically prefer/perform better with the LESS natural formant synthesis over diphone/natural speech at very fast rates. (4) STRONGEST CORROBORATION — the 2018 Journal of Speech Sciences study (Trouvain et al.) on time-compressed NATURAL utterances (9-19 syll/sec) found sighted subjects actually scored BETTER on intelligibility and blind only slightly better on acceptability, a result the authors explicitly note 'contradicts a trend often reported in the literature' — directly supporting that the blind advantage collapses for natural compressed speech. (5) Sources are peer-reviewed academic (not marketing/forum), span 2007-2018 in a stable psychoacoustics domain (not outdated). MINOR CAVEAT (does not refute): the 2008 fMRI single-case (Moos et al., amlap.org) found one exceptional blind subject who DID understand ultra-fast NATURAL speech at 16 syll/sec — so 'no transfer at all' is slightly overstated for elite individuals, and natural recognition was still lower than synthetic. But the core practical claim — that the 22 syll/sec ceiling is achieved with hyper-articulated synthetic TTS rather than compressed audiobook narration, and that the blind/sighted gap largely vanishes for natural speech — is confirmed by independent primary sources and is directly relevant to the user's screen-reader-TTS-vs-re-encoded-Audible tool choice.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/ (2013 training/fMRI study used exclusively formant-synth TTS, ~70% at 18 s/s) and https://econtents.sbu.unicamp.br/inpec/index.php/joss/article/view/14951 (2018 JoSS: sighted better on natural time-compressed intelligibility, blind only slightly better on acceptability) — both corroborate rather than refute the claim.

(2018, J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 144(4):2088, PMC6181647) replicated the U-shaped intelligibility recovery in both young and older adults (with only an age-related shift in the optimal packaging rate from 5 to 4 packets/sec). The narrow claim as written, however, is true and well-sourced.

result.evidence: All three sub-claims are verbatim supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (PLOS ONE 2017, Tzukernman/Karni et al., PMC5436740; n=65 native Hebrew speakers, 4 trained groups + control). No contradicting source found.

Primary source CONFIRMS the claim directly. PMC5436740 = Gabay, Karni & Banai (2017), "The perceptual learning of time-compressed speech: A comparison of training protocols with different levels of difficulty." Methods: a single session of ~1 hour, 5 blocks of 60 trials = 300 total trials, exactly as the claim states. Results: "all four protocols resulted in significant post-training improvement"; all four trained groups significantly outperformed the untrained control group on the repeated/trained condition (p < .01). No source disputes within-session learning; the field uniformly reports rapid early gains. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary psycholinguistic research, 2017, replicated finding) matches the modest, non-extraordinary claim. Minor caveat: post-training gains in the 2017 study were partly token/stimulus-specific and protocol-dependent (constant-low > constant-high), but this does not undercut the claim's core assertion (within-session gains; trained > minimally-exposed controls). Claim is well-supported, current, not marketing, and not outdated.

(3) Constant-high being worst is true for the headline TRAINED-ITEMS measure ("training in the constant-high compression condition resulted in significantly smaller gains also compared to the two adaptive training conditions"). Two minor, non-refuting qualifications: (a) the "worst of all trained groups" gap narrows on transfer — constant-high vs constant-low was non-significant on new tokens and only a p=.06 trend on new talkers; (b) authors hedge with "may be advantageous," but their "lack of a clearly advantageous protocol" caveat refers to GENERALIZATION across measures, not to the trained-items 91-vs-76 finding the claim cites. Contradicting "hard-to-easy" evidence exists only for perceptual-category-integration tasks, a different domain. The claim's numbers and core assertion are accurate, current (2017, slow-moving field), from a primary peer-reviewed source matching claim strength, and not contradicted for time-compressed speech. Survives refutation.
result.counterSource: No credible counter-source for this specific domain. Potential minor qualifier: link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13423-019-01627-4 (Hussain et al.) shows a "sweet spot" — too-easy initial trials reduce benefit — and notes some perceptual-category-integration studies favor hard-to-easy. These qualify generality but do not contradict the time-compressed-speech finding cited.

result.evidence: The raw numbers are accurate per the primary source (Banai & Lavner 2012, PLOS ONE / PMC3467231): 5 sessions over 10 days, ~300 sentences/session (5 adaptive blocks x 60 trials), two-down one-up staircase, Cohen's d = 1.37, time-compressed speech. BUT the claim's interpretive framing is an overreach/misread on three counts. (1) The d=1.37 was for the TRAINED CONDITION ONLY (same sentences, trained speaker). Per Table 2 and text, gains were largely stimulus-specific: they did NOT generalize to untrained sentences or single words (only to the same sentences spoken by different talkers). So it does not 'establish a concrete evidence-based ramp/overload schedule for improving time-compressed speech comprehension' generally. (2) The staircase was adaptive WITHIN sessions (compression modified logarithmically toward a 20% floor), not a documented progressive between-session 'ramp/overload' — calling it an overload ramp mischaracterizes the mechanism. (3) Population mismatch: subjects were non-native Arabic-speaking learners of Hebrew (vision status never assessed), so d=1.37 partly reflects non-native language-learning effects, not a sighted listener's general speed-listening ceiling gain in wpm/syllables-per-second. The figure also measures accuracy on a true/false sentence-verification task, not sustained high-rate comprehension. Legitimate primary peer-reviewed source, but the claim overstates narrow, stimulus-specific perceptual learning as a validated general training schedule.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3467231/ (Banai & Lavner 2012, Table 2 and Results — gains stimulus-specific, no generalization to untrained sentences/words; within-session adaptive staircase, not a between-session overload ramp; non-native Hebrew learners, vision status unassessed)

result.evidence: Claim is well-supported by the primary source and independently corroborated. The source is Banai & Lavner (2014), "The effects of training length on the perceptual learning of time-compressed speech and its generalization," JASA 136(4):1908 (PMID 25324090) — a peer-reviewed primary study, appropriate quality for the claim's strength. The abstract confirms exactly the claim: a brief-exposure group (20 sentences) and a single-session group improved on TRAINED material, but "only the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens." So brief exposure produced measurable learning that did NOT generalize to untrained tokens — precisely the claim. The supporting quote's opening "yields both learning and generalization" is the paper's overall framing (generalization came from the 3-session group), and the claim correctly carves out that BRIEF exposure specifically does not generalize — no overreach. A SECOND independent paper from the same group (Banai et al., "Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation," PLOS ONE 2012, PMC3467231) replicates the finding: "Learning from brief exposure was specific and did not generalize to stimuli not encountered during the initial exposure phase," whereas multiday training generalized across talkers. The 2019 Springer follow-up (s13414-019-01714-7) and 2017 PLOS ONE protocol paper are consistent. Not outdated (stable cognitive-psychology result), not marketing, not a press release or forum speculation. Practical implication for the user's question is sound: rapid gains from short exposure are stimulus-specific, so durable, transferable speed-listening improvement requires sustained multi-session training — consistent with the user's reported 4-month adaptation arc.
result.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found. The strongest potential "counter" (the quote's phrase "yields both learning and generalization") actually comes from the same paper and refers to the 3-session group, not brief exposure; the independent PLOS ONE 2012 paper (PMC3467231) affirms rather than contradicts that brief exposure does not generalize.

result.evidence: Primary source PMC3467231 ("Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation," PLoS ONE 2012, PubMed 23056592) confirms every load-bearing fact: "Five sessions were administered over the course of 10 days"; each session had "5 adaptive blocks of 60 trials" = 300 sentences; "A two-down one-up procedure was employed, with an initial compression level of 65%"; Table 2 reports Cohen's d = 1.37 for the trained group's pre-to-post gain on the trained condition; and the task was a semantic sentence-verification comprehension task on time-compressed speech. All claim numbers are exact and verifiable against a peer-reviewed primary source, so it is not refuted. Caveats that qualify but do not refute it: (1) participants were native-Arabic L2-Hebrew listeners (second-language, not native speed-listening), limiting generalization to the user's English use case; (2) the word "ramp/overload" is imprecise — the protocol was an ADAPTIVE within-session staircase keyed to individual performance, not a progressive day-to-day overload ramp; (3) d=1.37 is the within-group trained-condition gain, which the paper itself notes mixes durable perceptual learning with rapid adaptation ("More than Rapid Adaptation" means only part is durable). These are framing imperfections, not factual errors in the core protocol/effect-size claim.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3467231/ (primary source itself confirms the protocol and d=1.37; the only counter-nuance is that the same source frames the staircase as adaptive, not a progressive overload ramp, and notes the gain is partly rapid adaptation)

result.evidence: The claim MISREADS its own primary source (Banai & Lavner 2014, JASA 136(4):1908-17, PMID 25324090). The claim asserts brief exposure (20 sentences) produces learning that "does NOT generalize to untrained speech tokens." But the abstract's HEADLINE finding — present verbatim in the supporting quote — states the OPPOSITE: "Brief exposure to time-compressed speech yields both learning AND generalization." The claim cherry-picks only the second half of the quote ("only the group trained for three sessions outperformed... when tested with untrained tokens") and inverts it into "no generalization." The real finding is about DEGREE, not presence: brief exposure DOES generalize, but 3-session training generalizes MORE (it outperformed all other groups on untrained tokens). Flattening "generalizes less than 3 sessions" into "does not generalize at all" is a factual error. Part 1 of the claim (brief exposure produces learning) is supported; Part 2 (no generalization) is directly contradicted by the source. Note: the broader literature (e.g. Nature Sci Rep 2022, PMC9200863) characterizes this line of work as showing learning is largely STIMULUS/TALKER-SPECIFIC even after training — so the spirit of "specificity" has some support, but the specific claim as written ("brief exposure does NOT generalize to untrained tokens") is refuted by the paper's explicit statement that brief exposure yields BOTH learning and generalization. Source quality is fine (peer-reviewed primary study); the problem is the claim contradicts it. Also marginally outdated framing aside, the core issue is misrepresentation, not recency.
result.counterSource: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25324090/ (the claim's OWN source: abstract states "Brief exposure to time-compressed speech yields both learning and generalization"); corroborating literature characterization at https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9200863/

result.evidence: The source is genuine and the quote is accurate: Banai & Lavner (2014), "The effects of training length on the perceptual learning of time-compressed speech and its generalization" (PubMed 25324090), did find "only the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens." So the narrow directional finding (longer practice -> more STABLE generalization) is supported. HOWEVER, the claim's strong wording — "a single session is insufficient to produce transferable gains" — is an OVERREACH contradicted by the closely related primary literature: (a) Banai & Lavner's own foundational study "Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation" (PMC3467231) found that even brief pre/post exposure significantly improved performance on UNTRAINED sentences in BOTH trained and control groups, and that extended multi-session training paradoxically became MORE stimulus-specific rather than more generalized; (b) the classic Dupoux & Green (1997) work shows rapid within-session adaptation occurs in just 10-20 sentences and generalizes across compression rates and even to novel talkers. So single-session exposure DOES produce some transferable gains — three sessions adds stability/consistency, not the existence of transfer. Additionally, there is a major RELEVANCE gap to the research question: these studies measure recognition accuracy of degraded single sentences at ~35-45% compression (~2-3x) in non-native/hard-listening conditions; "untrained tokens" means new SENTENCES, not a HIGHER SPEED. They do not test an already-adapted native listener pushing from 3.5x toward 7-10x, so the inference "a progressive/longer training protocol is needed for real-world [speed-listening] improvement" is an extrapolation the cited study does not directly support. Refuted on overreach/misread grounds, though the weaker version (longer practice yields more stable speed-adaptation) is defensible.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3467231/ (Banai & Lavner 2012, "More than Rapid Adaptation" — brief exposure already generalized to untrained sentences; extended training became more specific); Dupoux & Green 1997 (PubMed 9701975 / related, rapid within-20-sentence adaptation generalizing across rates and talkers)

result.evidence: Source verified: Banai & Lavner (2014), "The effects of training length on the perceptual learning of time-compressed speech and its generalization," J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 136(4):1908-1917 (PMID 25324090). This is a peer-reviewed PRIMARY source studying exactly the speed-listening domain (TIME-COMPRESSED speech, not a tangential distortion). The claim faithfully paraphrases the abstract: listeners trained 1 or 3 sessions; "only the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens" and "more stable learning and generalization might be achieved with longer, multi-session practice." Corroborated by follow-up: a 2019 Attention, Perception & Psychophysics study (Springer s13414-019-01714-7) found "semantic transfer requires more than a single training session to emerge" and failed to replicate the small single-session transfer effects. Skeptical counter-check: a RELATED Banai et al. 2012 paper ("More than Rapid Adaptation," PMC3467231) shows brief 10-20 sentence exposure produces BROAD adaptation transfer across talkers/languages/compression rates, while longer training becomes paradoxically MORE stimulus-specific. This qualifies but does NOT contradict the claim, because (a) it concerns a different, very-short-term adaptation phenomenon along different transfer dimensions, and (b) it does not overturn the specific finding that untrained-TOKEN generalization in the training paradigm requires three sessions. The claim is appropriately hedged ("might be achieved"). Domain caveat for the parent report: this is sighted young adults, generic perceptual learning — supports a progressive/multi-session training protocol, but says nothing about reaching extreme 7x-10x ceilings.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3467231/ (Banai et al. 2012, "More than Rapid Adaptation") shows brief exposure generalizes broadly while longer training is more stimulus-specific — a qualification, not a refutation. Replication support: https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-019-01714-7 (2019) confirms semantic transfer needs more than one session.

result.evidence: The primary source (Banai & Lavner 2014, JASA 136(4):1908-1917, PMID 25324090) verbatim abstract directly supports the claim's load-bearing point. It explicitly tested a group "briefly exposed to 20 time-compressed sentences" against 1-session, 3-session, and naive groups, and found: "Trained listeners outperformed the other groups on the trained condition, but ONLY the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens." This confirms the claim's two core assertions: (a) 20-sentence brief exposure produces measurable learning, and (b) that learning does NOT generalize to untrained tokens (only 3 sessions did). The "20 sentences" detail is exact. The companion open-access paper (Banai & Lavner 2012, PMC3467231) corroborates that brief 10-20 sentence exposure produces learning that is stimulus-specific. ONE QUALIFICATION (not a refutation): the abstract's framing sentence "Brief exposure... yields both learning and generalization" and the 2012 work show brief adaptation DOES generalize to new TALKERS — so the claim's phrase "ONLY improves the specific trained material" is slightly overstated, since talker-generalization exists. But the claim is explicitly scoped to "untrained speech TOKENS," and on that precise variable the claim matches the results exactly. The misleadingly self-contradictory "supporting quote" attached to the claim was resolved by the claim-writer in the direction the actual data supports. Source is peer-reviewed primary research, current, corroborated, not marketing — quality matches claim strength.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3467231/ (Banai & Lavner 2012) notes brief adaptation generalizes to new talkers, mildly qualifying the "only specific trained material" phrasing — but does not contradict the untrained-token specificity that the claim asserts.

result.evidence: The primary source (PubMed 25324090, Banai & Lavner, "The effects of training length on the perceptual learning of time-compressed speech and its generalization") is genuine, peer-reviewed, and on-topic — it concerns time-compressed/accelerated speech, the exact speed-listening domain. The supporting quote matches the abstract verbatim: only the 3-session group outperformed others on UNTRAINED tokens. So the directional core (multi-session > single session for that specific generalization measure) IS supported. HOWEVER, the claim overreaches in stating "a single session is insufficient to produce transferable gains." The same research program contradicts this: (a) PMC3467231 (Banai & Lavner, "More than Rapid Adaptation") finds brief single-session exposure to just 10-20 sentences produces robust, long-lasting, TALKER-INDEPENDENT and CONTENT-INDEPENDENT generalization, and that EXTENDED multi-session training actually makes learning MORE stimulus-SPECIFIC ("learning progresses from an initial general phase to a subsequent prolonged and more stimulus-specific phase") — the opposite of the claim's framing that more sessions = more transfer. (b) PMC6200219 found typical (non-dyslexic) young adults generalized well to NEW SPEAKERS after a SINGLE session. Thus single-session training DOES produce transferable gains; the 3-session advantage is an incremental effect on one particular untrained-token comparison, not evidence that single sessions yield zero transfer. The claim's absolute phrasing ("single session is insufficient," "needed for real-world improvement") is an overreach beyond what the quote and surrounding literature support. Additionally, all this is lab-based sentence-verification accuracy on artificially accelerated speech, which does not directly establish a "real-world improvement" protocol claim.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3467231/ (Banai & Lavner, "Perceptual Learning of Time-Compressed Speech: More than Rapid Adaptation") — finds single-session/brief exposure yields talker- and content-independent generalization, while extended multi-session training becomes MORE stimulus-specific; and https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6200219/ — typical adults generalized to new speakers after a single session.

result.evidence: The literal text IS supported: the Frontiers 2013 paper (Dietrich et al.) states verbatim "normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s," and blind listeners reach ~22 syl/s in the same sentence, so "far below blind listeners' ceiling" is accurate. HOWEVER the claim presents "approximately 8 syllables per second" as a firm "documented upper limit," and this is overstated and contested: (1) The 8 syl/s figure is NOT the paper's own measurement — WebFetch of PMC3805979 confirms it is a secondary citation to Moos & Trouvain (2007); the authors themselves did not systematically test sighted listeners across rates. (2) Other credible sources give HIGHER numbers: Scientific American (covering the same research group) states "10 syllables per second, the absolute limit of comprehension for sighted people," and the research question's own established context cites "~11-16 syll/sec untrained sighted." So the literature spans 8 to 16 syll/sec, and the claim cherry-picks the lowest single value and frames it as definitive. The number is real and citable, but "the documented upper limit ... is approximately 8 syllables per second" overstates certainty for a contested, secondhand figure. Recommend rephrasing to a range (≈8–16 syll/sec depending on source) rather than asserting a hard 8 syl/s ceiling.
result.counterSource: Scientific American "Why Can Some Blind People Process Speech Far Faster Than Sighted Persons?" states the sighted absolute comprehension limit is 10 syl/s, not 8; research-question context cites 11-16 syll/sec untrained sighted; the 8 syl/s figure in the Frontiers paper is itself a secondary citation to Moos & Trouvain (2007), not an original measurement.

result.evidence: Claim is well-supported by the primary source and corroborated independently. The Frontiers article (Dietrich et al. 2013, fMRI training study of late-blind subjects) states verbatim: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s." This traces to the original Moos & Trouvain (2007, ICPhS) experiment ("vision loss of peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second"). Independent corroboration: BMC Neuroscience 2013 (PMC3847124) and PubMed both report blind listeners comprehending up to 22 syll/s vs ~8 syll/s for sighted. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary research) matches the claim strength; not outdated; not marketing. Minor caveat: the "22 syll/s" figure is an upper bound for trained/proficient late-blind listeners (the study's expert group), not a universal late-blind capacity — but the claim's wording ("up to approximately 22") already encodes this correctly. No contradicting source found.

The quote "whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s" is genuine in the cited Frontiers article (Dietrich et al. 2013, https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full), attributed to Moos & Trouvain (2007) — a credible primary source. The directional point (sighted ceiling << trained-blind ~22 syl/s) is sound. HOWEVER the claim overreaches in two ways: (1) It presents 8 syl/s as "THE documented upper limit... for normal-sighted listeners" but the source and broader literature describe this as the UNTRAINED maximum. Time-compressed-speech training research (PMC3467231; PMC2775905) shows sighted listeners improve markedly with only 8-10 hours of practice and comprehend well above 2x, so 8 syl/s is not an immutable sighted ceiling — it omits the load-bearing "untrained" qualifier. (2) The number conflicts with the literature's spread: the same Frontiers study's "ultra-fast" condition is 16 syl/s where sighted subjects still scored "below 20%" (i.e., non-zero comprehension above 8 syl/s), Wikipedia's time-compressed-speech literature reports sighted comprehension in WPM (200-250 wpm easy, higher with training) without endorsing a hard 8 syl/s wall, and the research question itself cites the operative untrained-sighted range as ~11-16 syll/sec. Thus 8 syl/s is a single-study figure at the low end, presented with false precision as a universal "upper limit of comprehension" — an overreach/under-qualified misuse of the quote.
result.counterSource: Frontiers/Dietrich et al. 2013 states sighted subjects at 16 syl/s scored "below 20%" (non-zero, so 8 is not a hard wall); PMC3467231 and PMC2775905 document sighted listeners trained to far exceed 2x compression in 8-10 hours; Wikipedia time-compressed-speech reports sighted comprehension 200-250+ wpm without an 8 syl/s ceiling; research-question framing cites untrained-sighted ~11-16 syll/sec.

result.evidence: Claim is well-supported by primary peer-reviewed sources and survives refutation attempts. The Frontiers in Human Neuroscience 2013 paper (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann, "Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans," PMC3805979) states verbatim: "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)" and "Individuals suffering from vision loss of a peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per seconds (s)—exceeding by far the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)." The claim's wording ("Trained late-blind individuals can comprehend... up to approximately 22 syllables per second") faithfully matches this, preserving the "up to approximately" upper-bound qualifier. CORROBORATION: Independent sources confirm the same figure — BMC Neuroscience 2013 fMRI study (bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74) reports blind participants comprehending up to 22 syl/s vs ~8 syl/s for sighted. The 22 syl/s figure traces to the foundational empirical study Moos & Trouvain (2007), "Comprehension of ultra-fast speech – blind vs. normally hearing persons." NO contradicting source found. NUANCE (not refuting): the 22 syl/s is an upper bound from prior work, not original data from this 2013 study, whose own late-blind participants trained at 18 syl/s and did not all reach the 80%-accuracy target — but the claim asserts the established ceiling, not this study's own results, so this is consistent. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary neuroscience) matches the claim's strength; finding is stable in a slow-moving subfield, not outdated; not marketing.

Claim is verbatim-supported by the primary source (Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013, Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann), titled "Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension... in late-blind humans": "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s." The claim's narrowing to "late-blind" and "trained" is faithful — the paper title and body explicitly address late-blind individuals and state the skill is acquired via training/repeated exposure to screen-reader TTS. INDEPENDENTLY CORROBORATED by a second peer-reviewed paper from the BMC Neuroscience fMRI study (PMC3847124): "repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables per second." Scientific American and other secondary coverage echo the same figure; some sources cite an even higher ~25 syl/s, so 22 is central/conservative rather than a cherry-picked extreme. No contradicting or dissenting source found. Source quality (peer-reviewed neuroscience) matches the claim's empirical strength; finding is stable, not outdated.

Primary peer-reviewed source (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann 2013, Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, cross-verified on PMC3805979) directly supports every substantive element. Exact quotes: baseline — "Prior to the training procedure, none of the subjects was able to understand ultra-fast speech at a level of 18 syl/s... the percentage of correctly reproduced syllables fell below 14% in all instances." Post-training — "Four subjects (nos. 147, 150, 144, and 142) showed an improved behavioral performance of up to ca. 70% points (mean = 71%, SE = 2.74%)." Pattern — "Four of the six participants showed... considerable enhancement of ultra-fast speech perception... whereas behavioral performance did not change in the two remaining participants." Training duration ~6 months at 18 syl/s confirmed. One minor imprecision: the 6 participants were 5 blind + 1 normal-sighted, not strictly "six late-blind" as the claim states, but the paper's own narrative frames them as late-blind subjects and the 4-of-6 / below-14%-to-~70% / 18-syl/s figures are all exactly correct. Not outdated, not marketing, not forum speculation; source quality fully matches claim strength.

The quantitative facts are accurate, but the population label is WRONG. The claim says "four of six LATE-BLIND participants" improved. The primary source (Dietrich, Hertrich, Ackermann 2013, Front. Hum. Neurosci.) explicitly states the sample was "Five blind subjects and a single normal-sighted individual." The four improvers are subjects 147, 150, 144, and 142 — and subject 142 is the SOLE NORMAL-SIGHTED participant, not late-blind. So only THREE of the four improvers were late-blind; the fourth was sighted. The study notes improvement occurred "independently from residual visual functions." Confirmed numbers: training "over a period of ca. 6 months" (~1h/day with JAWS screen reader); baseline "the percentage of correctly reproduced syllables fell below 14% in all instances" at 18 syl/s; post-training "up to ca. 70% points correctly repeated words"; ultra-fast rate = 18 syl/s. The mislabel is material to this research question: the study is actually rare direct evidence that a SIGHTED person reached ~70% comprehension at 18 syl/s after training — exactly the sighted-ceiling question — which the claim's "late-blind" framing erases. Misread of source population; not fully supported as worded.

result.evidence: The verbatim quote is real and correctly attributed (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann 2013, Frontiers Hum Neurosci; figure originally from Moos & Trouvain 2007). BUT the claim overreaches by dropping the load-bearing qualifier "UNTRAINED." The same authors' companion fMRI paper (BMC Neuroscience 2013, PMC3847124) states it verbatim as "the upper limits of UNTRAINED subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)." So 8 syl/s is the UNTRAINED-sighted baseline, NOT a "documented upper limit of comprehension for normal-sighted listeners" as a fixed ceiling, as the claim implies. This is directly contradicted by the SAME source's own data: in Dietrich et al. 2013 (PMC3805979, Table 1), a normally-sighted control (Subject 142) trained on ultra-fast speech improved from 0% to 67% correctly repeated words at 18 syl/s — more than DOUBLE the supposed 8 syl/s "upper limit." The broader literature (Foulke & Sticht; the research question's own "~11-16 syll/sec untrained sighted" range) likewise shows the figure is method/material-dependent, not a hard ceiling. For a research question specifically about a TRAINED sighted listener, presenting 8 syl/s as "the documented upper limit ... for normal-sighted listeners" is misleading: it conflates the untrained baseline with the trained/physiological ceiling, and the source itself disproves the implied limit. Two independent grounds (misread/overreach + contradiction by the same source's training data) meet the refutation bar.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/ (Dietrich et al. 2013, BMC Neuroscience: "the upper limits of untrained subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)") and https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/ (Dietrich et al. 2013, Table 1: sighted control Subject 142 reached 67% comprehension at 18 syl/s after training)

result.evidence: VERIFIED against the primary source (Dietrich, Hertrich & Ackermann 2013, Front. Hum. Neurosci., PMC3805979). The core quantitative claim is accurate: (a) Training ~6 months — "ca. 6 months." (b) Baseline <14% — "the percentage of correctly reproduced syllables fell below 14% in all instances" at 18 syl/s. (c) Four of six improved to ~70% — "Four of six participants showed... a significant improvement of ultra-fast (18 syl/s) speech comprehension, in terms of a performance level of ca. 70% points" / "four subjects (nos. 147, 150, 144, and 142) showed an improved behavioral performance of up to ca. 70% points." (d) Rate 18 syl/s — confirmed. Source is a primary peer-reviewed study; strength matches. ONE factual error in the claim: it says "four of six LATE-BLIND participants," but the study had FIVE blind subjects plus ONE normal-sighted individual (six total), and subject 142 — one of the four improvers — was the normal-sighted control (post-training ~67% at 18 syl/s). So the four improvers were three late-blind + one sighted, NOT four late-blind. This mischaracterizes participant composition but does NOT contradict the substantive numbers (4/6, <14%->~70%, 18 syl/s, ~6 mo), which are all correct. Note: "70% points" = absolute percentage-point performance level (~70% correct), not a 70-point gain on top of 70%. The misattribution is a minor overreach, not grounds to kill the claim; if anything the sighted improver (142) makes this MORE relevant to the research question's sighted-ceiling inquiry. Not outdated (2013 remains the definitive primary study on trained ultra-fast listening; not a fast-moving field). Not marketing/forum. Recommend the synthesis correct "late-blind" to "blind-and-sighted (five blind + one sighted; one sighted subject among the four improvers)."
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/ — "Five blind subjects and a single normal-sighted individual... participated"; subject 142 = the sighted control, one of the four improvers, contradicting the claim's "four LATE-BLIND participants" wording.

result.evidence: The claim is well-supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (O'Leary, Neukam, Hansen, Kinney, Capach, Svirsky & Wingfield, 2023, "Strategic Pauses Relieve Listeners from the Effort of Listening to Fast Speech," Trends in Hearing, SAGE, doi:10.1177/23312165231203514; PMID 37941344). The PubMed abstract explicitly confirms Experiment 1 used 27 normal-hearing young adults as a proof-of-concept that "restoring lost processing time by inserting silent pauses at linguistically salient points within a time-compressed narrative ('time-restoration') returns recall accuracy to a level approximating that for a normal speech rate." Mechanism is confirmed: speech was time-compressed to 60% of original playing time (fast syllable rate retained) and only silence was inserted at clause/sentence boundaries — pupillometry showed effort was reduced with time-restoration. This is NOT contradicted anywhere; it sits atop a 25-year converging literature: the foundational Wingfield, Tun, Koh & Rosen (1999, Psychology and Aging, PMID 10509694) established time-restoration improves recall and that pauses at linguistic boundaries beat random placement, replicated/extended by O'Leary & Wingfield (2024) and Frontiers (2023). Source quality matches claim strength. ONE precision gap lowering confidence: the exact figures "~95% recovery" and "p=.874" could not be independently verified in retrieved sources (the abstract says recall "approximating" normal rate, which is qualitatively consistent with "statistically indistinguishable," but the specific p-value was not surfaced outside the claim's own supporting material). result.evidence: The empirical core is real and well-sourced (Scientific Reports 2023 / PMC9938863, 14 normal-hearing young adults, mean 22.3 yrs): syllables of speech compressed to 15.6 sps were manually segmented, 100ms silence added after each syllable to restore ~6.1 sps, yielding a significant intelligibility gain (main effect F(1,13)=52.53, p<0.00001). So "significantly improves intelligibility" is supported, and the quote literally says "for all of the spectral content conditions." HOWEVER the claim contains two defects. (a) "across ALL spectral conditions" is misleading: the benefit was strongly NON-uniform and TFS-dependent — 23.40% (original), 16.46% (16-ch vocoder), but only 4.97% (8-ch vocoder); the paper's own conclusion is that "the benefit associated with repackaging TC speech depended on the amount of TFS available," not a uniform across-the-board effect. (b) The load-bearing interpretive gloss — "a throughput technique that raises comprehension WITHOUT lowering the underlying syllable production rate" — misrepresents the mechanism and is unsupported by the quote. Repackaging ADDS silence, dropping delivery from 15.6 sps to 6.1 sps and INCREASING total listening time; net information throughput (words/minute) goes DOWN versus the silence-free compressed baseline. The paper demonstrates the auditory system needs theta-rhythm packaging time to decode, i.e. you trade time for intelligibility — the opposite of a throughput gain. For the user's stated goal (increase throughput, '7x'/'10x'), citing this as a throughput-raising trick is an overreach: it is an intelligibility-restoration trick that costs net speed. Source quality is high (primary, peer-reviewed, current 2023), so the refutation is on overreach/misframing, not source quality.

result.evidence:
The factual CORE of the claim is correct and well-sourced, but the claim's load-bearing FRAMING ("a throughput technique that raises comprehension without lowering the underlying syllable production rate") is an overreach not supported by the quote and contradicted by the study's own numbers.

WHAT IS SUPPORTED: The primary source (Koning et al., "The importance of temporal-fine structure to perceive time-compressed speech with and without the restoration of the syllabic rhythm," Nature Scientific Reports 2023; PMC9938863) does state verbatim: "There was a significant increase in intelligibility when the speech rate was restored to 6.1 sps by adding silences and that for all of the spectral content conditions (p <= 0.005)." Method: 100 ms silence added after each syllable, concatenated; 14 normal-hearing young adults (mean 22.3y), Dutch sentences, three spectral conditions (original, 16-ch and 8-ch noise vocoder). Improvements: +4.97% (8-ch), +16.46% (16-ch), +23.40% (original). So the intelligibility-across-all-conditions fact is genuinely true and primary-sourced.

WHY THE CLAIM IS AN OVERREACH (the part that matters for this research question): The study compressed speech to 15.6 sps and then "restored" it to 6.1 sps by inserting 100 ms after EACH syllable. 6.1 sps is the ORIGINAL/normal sentence rate for this corpus. Adding 100 ms to ~64 ms syllables (1/15.6 s) yields ~164 ms/syllable = 6.1 sps total — i.e., total audio duration is dragged back up to roughly NORMAL-speech duration. So the repackaged audio plays at the SAME words-per-minute as normal speech and is ~2.5x SLOWER in elapsed time than the compressed version. Net information throughput (wpm) is NOT increased over normal speech and is REDUCED versus the compressed audio. Characterizing this as a "throughput technique" that "raises comprehension without lowering the underlying syllable production rate" is misleading: the claim exploits the technically-true fact that intra-syllable production rate stays at 15.6 sps to imply a throughput benefit, but the user-relevant metric (wpm / elapsed time) is restored to normal. The supporting quote says nothing about throughput preservation; that gloss is the claimant's editorializing.

CONTEXT NUANCE: A sibling Ghitza study (PMC4082093, 2014) found optimal repackaging at ~9 packets/sec (theta-max ~9 Hz), where repackaged speech IS faster than normal conversational speech — so SOME repackaging configurations do retain a modest net-speed advantage. But the SPECIFIC source and figure cited in this claim (6.1 sps) is a restoration to the corpus's normal rate, giving zero throughput gain. The claim cites the wrong study to support a throughput argument.

VERDICT for the user's question (seeking 7x-10x throughput): repackaging to 6.1 sps would put playback at NORMAL speed (~150 wpm baseline), far below their current sustained 3.5x — it is a comprehension-aid for the COMPRESSED segments, not a way to exceed current throughput. The claim overstates its utility as a throughput technique.

VERIFIED, not refuted. The primary source (PMC9938863 = "The importance of temporal-fine structure to perceive time-compressed speech with and without the restoration of the syllabic rhythm," Scientific Reports / Nature portfolio, peer-reviewed, 2023) directly states: "Post-hoc analyses showed that there was a significant increase in intelligibility when the speech rate was restored to 6.1 sps by adding silences and that for all of the spectral content conditions (p ≤ 0.005)." Confirmed details: speech was time-compressed to 15.6 sps, manually segmented into syllables, 100ms silence added after each syllable to restore 6.1 sps; 14 normal-hearing young adults; all three spectral conditions (original, 16-channel, 8-channel vocoding) reached significance, with gains of 23.40% (original), 16.46% (16-ch), 4.97% (8-ch). The "repackaging" finding is independently corroborated by Ghitza & Greenberg 2009 (gaps effective between ~20-120ms) and the TEMPO neural-oscillation framework — a robust, replicated result, not a one-off. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary research) matches claim strength; not outdated (2023); not marketing. CAVEAT on the claim's framing: the phrase "raises comprehension WITHOUT lowering the underlying syllable production rate" is partially misleading — adding silences DOES lower the effective/overall delivery rate (15.6→6.1 sps) and increases total duration, so it is NOT a free throughput gain. Only the within-segment articulation rate (15.6 sps) is preserved. Also, restoration is PARTIAL (not full) and depends on temporal-fine structure availability. But the literal, central assertion — 100ms silences restoring ~6.1 sps significantly improve intelligibility across all spectral conditions — is accurately supported by the verbatim quote.
result.counterSource: No contradicting source found; finding is corroborated by Ghitza & Greenberg 2009 (PMC figure/ResearchGate) and replicated in this 2023 Scientific Reports paper. Only nuance: the claim's "without lowering throughput" framing overstates the benefit, since restoring 6.1 sps increases total listening time vs the 15.6 sps compressed version.

result.evidence: Verified against the primary source (GitHub repo ywong137/speech-speed) and its raw README. Both confirm exactly: "Max speed (1.5-5x, default 3.5): ceiling for playback rate. Protects against extreme speedup on very slow speakers." Default ceiling = 3.5x (equal to Audible's cap); configurable range tops out at 5x; the extension does not support speeds above 5x. The claim's three assertions all hold: (a) default max is 3.5x — supported verbatim; (b) configurable up to 5x — supported verbatim; (c) "not a high-multiplier (7x-10x) solution" — directly entailed since 5x is the hard maximum, below the user's 7x acceptability threshold. The claim is NOT an overreach: it explicitly acknowledges the 5x upper bound ("only modestly exceeds it at the upper bound") rather than overstating. Additional corroboration: the extension is a speech-NORMALIZING tool (speeds up slow speakers, slows fast ones to a comfortable target), not a throughput-maximizer, and a separate algorithmic detection cap (~70ms minimum crossing interval ≈ ~14 syllables/sec) further limits practical speedup — both reinforce that it is not designed for 7x-10x use. Source quality is sufficient (primary repo is authoritative for a tool-spec claim) and current (covered by TechPP March 2026). I attempted to refute on overreach, misread-quote, contradiction, source-quality, and outdatedness; none succeeded.

The claim is well-supported by the primary source (github.com/ywong137/speech-speed README) and corroborated by independent sources. (1) "Default max 3.5x": confirmed verbatim — README states "maxSpeed (1.5-5x, default 3.5): ceiling for playback rate. Protects against extreme speedup on very slow speakers." (2) "Configurable up to 5x": confirmed by the "(1.5-5x)" range. (3) "Does not natively exceed Audible's 3.5x cap by default": correct — the factory default of 3.5x equals Audible's 3.5x cap. (4) "Only modestly exceeds at the upper bound": correct — the absolute hard ceiling is 5x, and the README frames the tool around intelligibility/"consume video content faster without it becoming unintelligible," NOT extreme acceleration. (5) "Not a high-multiplier (7x-10x) solution": correct — 5x hard cap < 7x, and the stated design purpose is to "protect against extreme speedup," so it structurally cannot reach the user's 7x/10x goals. Corroboration: TechPP (2026-03-20) and a Hacker News thread (item 47361251) independently describe the same extension and its speed-normalization (not speed-maximization) purpose. Source is current (March 2026 coverage), primary, and the claim is appropriately hedged. No contradicting evidence found. The dynamic-normalization detail (it multiplies a speaker-normalized base) does not change the conclusion: the absolute playback-rate cap remains 5x, reinforcing that it is not a 7x-10x tool.
result.counterSource: No credible counter-source found. WebSearch (TechPP 2026-03-20, Hacker News item 47361251) and the raw README all corroborate the 3.5x-default / 5x-max figures and the normalization-not-extreme-speed positioning. Three attempts to refute (claim-vs-quote fit, contradicting search, source-strength) all failed to overturn it.

result.evidence: VERIFIED against primary source (raw README, github.com/ywong137/speech-speed, created 2026-03-13, updated 2026-06-16 — current as of the 2026-06-21 research date). The README states verbatim: "dynamically adjusts video playback speed based on how fast the speaker is talking. Slow speakers get sped up more; fast speakers get sped up less. The goal is to normalize all speech to a comfortable listening rate." Config: "Target rate (4-14 syl/s, default 9): the desired effective syllable rate you hear." The speed-mapping table confirms genuine variable-rate behavior (target 9 syl/s): very slow ~2.5 syl/s -> 3.50x, slow ~3.0 -> 3.00x, normal ~4.5 -> 2.00x, fast ~6.0 -> 1.50x, very fast ~8.0 -> 1.12x. The actual algorithm proves it is NOT a fixed multiplier: targetSpeed = targetSyllableRate / naturalRate, where naturalRate is measured live via energy-envelope zero-crossing detection. Independent corroboration: TechPP (2026-03-20) and Hacker News thread 47361251 describe the same mechanism with no dispute. CAVEAT (does not refute): "constant" is idealized — detection caps ~14 syl/s, min/max speed clamps (1.0/3.5) mean very slow speakers hit the 3.5x cap before reaching 9 syl/s, and EMA smoothing means speed converges toward rather than rigidly holds the target. The README itself frames this as "roughly the target rate," so the claim accurately reflects documented behavior rather than overreaching. Survives all 5 refutation attempts: well-supported by quote, no contradicting source, primary-source quality matching a descriptive (non-extraordinary) claim, current, and not marketing fluff (open-source code is inspectable).

The primary source (Deoisres et al., PLOS ONE, 2023-07-27, "Continuous speech with pauses inserted between words increases cortical tracking of speech envelope," DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0289288) verbatim supports the claim. Abstract: "Whether the increased cortical response, when longer pauses are introduced, reflect improved intelligibility requires further investigation." Discussion reinforces: "the increase in cortical tracking when pauses were added to speech does not necessarily imply that the participants have improved speech understanding." The claim is a faithful paraphrase — and notably a CAUTIOUS/hedging claim asserting that the comprehension benefit is NOT confirmed by neural tracking alone, which the source explicitly states. Confirmed against both the PLOS ONE article and the PMC mirror (PMC10374040). Checklist: (1) quote matches verbatim, no overreach; (2) no contradicting source found — indeed the paper notes aggressive pause-insertion (166ms every 333ms) REDUCED intelligibility, strengthening the decoupling of tracking from comprehension; (3) peer-reviewed primary source, appropriate for a methodological-caution claim needing no extraordinary evidence; (4) current (2023); (5) not marketing/forum/cherry-pick — academic primary literature. Caveat: the claim correctly does NOT assert pauses help comprehension — it only states the buffer-drain/pause benefit is unproven by neural tracking, which is exactly what the source says. Cannot be refuted.

The claim is well-supported, current, and properly sourced. VERIFIED QUOTE: The primary source (PLOS ONE 2023, Schmidt et al., "Continuous speech with pauses inserted between words increases cortical tracking of speech envelope," doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0289288, PMID 37498891) explicitly states: "Whether the increased cortical response, when longer pauses are introduced, reflect improved intelligibility requires further investigation." The supporting quote matches the source verbatim, so the claim is NOT an overreach — it accurately reports the authors' own stated limitation. STUDY DETAILS confirm the gap: the study measured EEG speech-envelope reconstruction (delta/theta bands) for natural speech vs. 250ms/500ms inserted pauses; pauses increased cortical tracking, but the authors collected NO usable behavioral comprehension data (comprehension questions were "unsuitable to be related to cortical responses" because identical across conditions). So the neural finding genuinely does not establish a comprehension/intelligibility benefit. CORROBORATION (strengthens, not refutes): Independent literature confirms cortical envelope tracking dissociates from intelligibility — e.g., "Neural tracking of speech envelope does not unequivocally reflect intelligibility" (NeuroImage 2023, PMID 36935084), and multiple sources note robust cortical tracking for COMPLETELY UNINTELLIGIBLE speech (noise-vocoded, time-reversed/backward speech). This means tracking-alone cannot prove comprehension, exactly as claimed. COUNTER-EVIDENCE CHECK: A separate line of work (Listening to the sound of silence, Neuropsychologia 2010) found silent pauses can REDUCE intelligibility when they make up a large fraction of speech, while improving word memory — but this does not contradict the claim; it actually reinforces that the buffer-drain/pause benefit for understanding is unconfirmed and complex. SOURCE QUALITY: peer-reviewed primary research (PLOS ONE) plus corroborating peer-reviewed neuroscience; appropriate for the claim's modest, hedged strength. NOT outdated (2023), not marketing/forum speculation. The claim makes no extraordinary positive assertion — it correctly states absence of confirmation. No grounds to refute.
result.counterSource: Neuropsychologia 2010 (PMID 20950633) found inserted silent pauses can reduce intelligibility while aiding word memory; and pure cortical-tracking studies cannot positively confirm a comprehension benefit. Neither contradicts the claim — both reinforce that any buffer-drain/pause benefit for understanding remains unconfirmed by neural tracking alone.

result.evidence: Claim VERIFIED against primary source (PLOS One 2023, pone.0289288, "Continuous speech with pauses inserted between words increases cortical tracking of speech envelope"). Supporting quote is verbatim: "Whether the increased cortical response, when longer pauses are introduced, reflect improved intelligibility requires further investigation." The paper goes further than the claim, explicitly stating "the increase in cortical tracking when pauses were added to speech does not necessarily imply that the participants have improved speech understanding" and "cortical tracking of speech envelope alone may not be an ideal measure to indicate how well a person can understand speech." Critically, the study had NO behavioral comprehension data: "Due to the lack of behavioural data in the current study, a clear conclusion on how the increase in cortical tracking when pauses were added to speech relates to individual's speech comprehension cannot be drawn." This is the strongest possible basis for the claim's hedge. No contradicting evidence found; instead independent literature CORROBORATES it: a NeuroImage 2023 paper is literally titled "Neural tracking of speech envelope does not unequivocally reflect intelligibility," and other 2023-2024 work shows envelope tracking is confounded by attention and listening effort, dissociating it from comprehension. Source is primary, peer-reviewed, on-topic, current (2023). The claim is properly scoped and well-hedged ("not confirmed by neural tracking alone"), matching source strength. Survives all five refutation checks. Note: this also implies the user-facing buffer-drain/pause benefit for comprehension is unproven at the behavioral level — which is itself an honest, useful caveat for the research question, not an overreach.
result.counterSource: None — search for contradicting evidence instead returned corroborating sources (NeuroImage 2023 "Neural tracking of speech envelope does not unequivocally reflect intelligibility"; Cortex 2024 listening-effort study; Cerebral Cortex 2023 sEEG dissociation study).

result.summary: No — 7x and especially 10x are not physically achievable as raw syllable-rate increases for any human, sighted or blind. The user's current 3.5x of slow Audible narration (~150 wpm) is already ~13 syllables/second, sitting at the very top of the documented untrained-sighted comprehension band (~9-16 syll/sec, with intelligibility collapsing from ~100% at 6.6 sps to ~42% at 15.6 sps). The documented human ceiling is ~22 syll/sec, achieved ONLY by trained late-blind listeners using hyper-articulated formant-synthesized TTS (not compressed natural audiobook narration), and even that took ~6 months of daily training plus visual-cortex plasticity — and this advantage largely vanishes for natural compressed speech. A literal "7x" of the slow baseline would be ~26 syll/sec and "10x" ~37 syll/sec, both far beyond any documented human comprehension. The realistic path to higher effective throughput is NOT a higher multiplier but (a) sustained multi-session perceptual-learning training with a progressive easy-to-hard ramp to push from ~13 toward the ~15-18 syll/sec edge, and (b) throughput tricks that raise information density without raising syllable rate — chiefly trimming silence and inserting strategic silent gaps at clause/sentence boundaries ("time-restoration"), which restored normal-hearing recall to ~95% of normal-rate baseline even while words stayed time-compressed.

findings.claim: 7x and 10x are NOT physically achievable as raw syllable-rate multipliers. The user's 3.5x (~13 syll/sec) already sits at the top of the untrained-sighted band; the absolute human ceiling (~22 syll/sec, trained blind) corresponds to only ~6x of the slow Audible baseline and is unattainable by sighted listeners with compressed natural speech.
findings.evidence: Trained late-blind listeners reach up to ~22 syll/sec vs a normal sighted upper limit of ~8 syll/sec (Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann 2013, citing Moos & Trouvain 2007). Untrained normal-hearing adults' intelligibility collapses from 99.8% at 6.6 sps to 41.9% at 15.6 sps (PMC9938863, 2023). Translating the user's multipliers against the slow ~150 wpm / ~3.7 syll-sec-equivalent baseline: 3.5x ~= 13 syll/sec (already top of the 9-16 untrained band); 7x ~= ~26 syll/sec; 10x ~= ~37 syll/sec. Even the absolute documented ceiling of 22 syll/sec is only ~6x of the slow baseline, so 7x exceeds ALL documented human comprehension and 10x is far beyond it. The 22 syll/sec feat is also blind+synthetic-TTS-specific, not available for compressed audiobook narration. NOTE: two claims that pushed the strongest 'physically impossible' framing were split-voted (1-2) on technicalities of derivation, but the unanimous claims independently establish the same ceiling.

findings.claim: The documented ~16-22 syll/sec ceiling is achieved ONLY by trained late-blind listeners using hyper-articulated formant-synthesized TTS, NOT compressed natural audiobook narration; the blind advantage largely collapses for natural compressed speech, where sighted listeners perform comparably or even better. This directly bears on tool choice: screen-reader/formant TTS, not re-encoded Audible audio, is the medium where the highest rates are even theoretically reachable.
findings.evidence: Moos & Trouvain (2007): 'the extra-ordinary listening skills found for formant synthetic speech is not found for natural speech in very fast modes.' The 2013 Frontiers training study used exclusively formant-synthesized JAWS 'Eloquence' TTS and reached only ~70% comprehension at 18 syll/sec. A 2018 Journal of Speech Sciences study on time-compressed NATURAL utterances (9-19 syll/sec) found sighted subjects scored BETTER on intelligibility and blind only slightly better on acceptability. One exceptional blind individual understood ultra-fast natural speech at 16 syll/sec, so transfer is not strictly zero for elite individuals, but the rule holds. Practical implication: the user's re-encoded Audible audio is natural speech, the medium where the blind/sighted gap nearly vanishes and the absolute ceiling is lower than the synthetic-TTS 22 syll/sec figure.

findings.claim: Best evidence-based training method: use a PROGRESSIVE easy-to-hard ramp (adaptive-incremental staircase), never severe compression from the start. A progressive ramp reached 91% word accuracy at 30% compression where a constant-high group reached only 76%; the constant-high (start-hard) group performed worst of all trained groups.
findings.evidence: Gabay/Karni/Banai (PLoS One 2017, PMC5436740), 65 normal-hearing sighted adults: 'Training procedures that started off with relatively little signal distortion were advantageous compared to severe distortions presented from the very beginning'; adaptive-incremental M=.91 vs constant-high M=.76 at the matched 30%-compression block. This is a concrete overload-scheduling recommendation directly relevant to a sighted listener: ramp difficulty gradually rather than jumping to maximum speed.

findings.claim: Concrete evidence-based training schedule and timeline: measurable gains appear within a single ~1-hour session (5 blocks of 60 trials = 300 items), but durable, transferable improvement requires multiple sessions. Learning proceeds in two phases — a rapid initial/general phase then a prolonged stimulus-specific phase — with most gain between sessions 1 and 2 and group-level asymptote after ~2 sessions. A documented protocol of 5 sessions over 10 days (300 sentences/session, two-down one-up adaptive staircase) produced a large effect (Cohen's d=1.37).
findings.evidence: PMC5436740: '~one hour... 5 blocks of 60 trials,' trained > minimally-exposed controls. PMC3467231 (Banai & Lavner 2012): two-phase learning, 'thresholds improved significantly between the first two training sessions... learning asymptotes after two training sessions,' 5 sessions/10 days/300 sentences, two-down one-up staircase from 65% compression, d=1.37 for trained pre/post. Caveat: d=1.37 is the pre/post trained-condition difference (within-training first-vs-last was d=0.56), and the measure was sentence-verification threshold, not wpm/recall. Practical ramp: short daily adaptive-staircase sessions, expect a fast initial bump then slower grind, plan for multiple sessions to consolidate.

findings.claim: Rapid gains from brief practice are stimulus-specific and do NOT generalize; only sustained multi-session training transfers to new, untrained material. This explains why genuine speed-listening improvement is a months-long arc rather than an instant skill, and matches the user's reported 4-month adaptation.
findings.evidence: Banai & Lavner (JASA 2014, PMID 25324090): brief exposure (20 sentences) and single-session groups improved on TRAINED material, but 'only the group that was trained for three sessions outperformed the other groups when tested with untrained tokens.' Replicated by PMC3467231: 'Learning from brief exposure was specific and did not generalize.' Note: a standalone claim that '3 sessions are strictly required for any transfer' was REFUTED (1-2) as over-precise, but the directional finding (transfer needs sustained multi-session training) is well-supported.

findings.claim: Documented training outcome / realistic gain magnitude: after ~6 months of daily training, four of six participants improved from <14% to ~70% comprehension at 18 syll/sec. Critically, one of the four improvers was the study's NORMAL-SIGHTED control subject (reaching ~67% at 18 syll/sec), providing the single documented data point of a sighted listener trained toward the ultra-fast range.
findings.vote: claim 12, split 2-1 (numbers verified; participant label corrected)
findings.evidence: Dietrich/Hertrich/Ackermann (Front. Hum. Neurosci. 2013, PMC3805979): ~6 months, baseline <14%, 'four subjects showed an improved behavioral performance of up to ca. 70% points' at 18 syll/sec. IMPORTANT CORRECTION from verification: the study had five blind subjects + one sighted control (six total); subject 142 among the four improvers was the SIGHTED control (~67% at 18 syll/sec). So a sighted person reaching ~18 syll/sec with synthetic TTS is documented once — but it required ~6 months of intensive daily training and used formant-synthesized speech, not natural narration. The earlier-circulated claim that this sighted subject hit a hard ceiling and could not improve further was REFUTED (0-3).

findings.claim: Highest-leverage throughput trick that raises information without raising syllable rate: insert strategic silent pauses at clause and sentence boundaries ('time-restoration'). This restored normal-hearing young adults' recall to ~95% of normal-rate baseline (statistically indistinguishable, p=.874) even though words stayed at the fast compressed syllable rate, and reduced measured listening effort (pupil dilation).
findings.evidence: O'Leary et al., Trends in Hearing 2023 (DOI 10.1177/23312165231203514, PMC10637151): time-restoration 'raised recall accuracy... 95% of the way back to baseline... did not differ significantly (p=.874).' Mechanism is cognitive (resource-limited), not perceptual: fast speech 'deprive[s] the listener of ordinarily available processing time'; pupillometry showed reduced effort with restoration. Optimal scheme: silence ONLY at linguistic boundaries (not uniform), sentence-boundary pauses = 2x clause-boundary pauses, total added silence calibrated so the restored passage matches the original uncompressed duration. Caveat: the ~95%/p=.874 figure is the clear-speech condition; benefit weakened under degraded (vocoded) audio. This is the single most actionable lever for the user: keep syllable rate high but reinsert boundary pauses to let comprehension 'catch up.'

findings.claim: Second throughput mechanism (theta-band 'repackaging'): heavily time-compressed speech (3x) drops below 50% words-correct, but inserting periodic ~80 ms silent gaps restores intelligibility, following a U-shaped error curve. Periodic insertion beats aperiodic; the optimum re-establishes a ~9 Hz (high-theta) syllabic packaging rate that drains/re-syncs the perceptual buffer.
findings.evidence: Ghitza & Greenberg (Phonetica 2009, PMID 19390234): 3x compression <50% words correct; 'lowest word error rate occurred when the silence was 80 ms long and inserted periodically'; U-shaped curve, intelligibility restored for gaps 20-120 ms; aperiodic insertion raised error. Caveat: the exact 80 ms optimum is specific to the TTS + 40-ms-fragment paradigm (Ghitza 2014 got 96% at 3x with natural syllabic segmentation), so it is not a universal 'insert 80 ms anywhere' recipe — but the gap-insertion/repackaging mechanism (restore a ~theta-band packaging rate) is robust. A specific claim about 100 ms inter-syllable gaps helping across all spectral conditions was REFUTED (1-2).

findings.claim: Caution on the buffer-drain/pause mechanism: increased neural (cortical envelope) tracking from inserted pauses does NOT by itself prove improved comprehension, and aggressive pause insertion can REDUCE intelligibility. The behavioral benefit (the time-restoration recall result above) is the reliable evidence, not neural-tracking metrics.
findings.evidence: Deoisres et al. (PLOS ONE 2023, DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0289288): 'Whether the increased cortical response... reflect improved intelligibility requires further investigation'; aggressive insertion (166 ms every 333 ms) reduced intelligibility. Implication: pause-insertion helps via giving cognitive catch-up time at the RIGHT (boundary) locations and durations, but over-fragmenting hurts — tune conservatively.

findings.claim: Content-aware variable-rate playback (normalizing all speech to a constant target syllable rate) is a real, deployed technique but is NOT a high-multiplier solution. The 'speech-speed' Chrome extension speeds slow speakers more and fast speakers less to hit a default 9 syll/sec target, yet caps at 3.5x default / 5x max — below the user's 7x-10x goals and only modestly above Audible's 3.5x.
findings.evidence: github.com/ywong137/speech-speed README: estimates speaker syllable rate via 2-10 Hz envelope modulation and normalizes to 'default 9 syl/s. Slow speakers get sped up more; fast speakers get sped up less'; 'Max speed (1.5-5x, default 3.5).' Confirms the right strategy (target a constant comfortable syllable rate rather than a blind multiplier — fast narrators don't need 3.5x, slow ones can take more) but also confirms no off-the-shelf consumer tool delivers 7x-10x because such rates exceed human comprehension regardless of player capability.

result.caveats: TOOLS COVERAGE IS THIN: the verified claim set robustly covers the science (sighted ceiling, training protocols, gap/pause throughput tricks) but the only concrete tool documented is the speech-speed Chrome extension (caps at 5x). The research did NOT surface verified evidence on custom players that exceed Audible's 3.5x via Rubber Band/SoundTouch re-encoding, on screen-reader TTS rates achievable in practice, or on community/practitioner (blind power-user, r/speedlistening) knowledge — claims 4 and 5 of the original brief are under-evidenced here and should be treated as open. BASELINE ARITHMETIC: 'x' translations depend on the assumed baseline. Audible '1x' ~150 wpm slow narration ~= ~3.5-3.9 syll/sec (English averages ~1.4 syll/word); normal conversation is ~4-6 syll/sec / ~180-220 wpm. So the user's 3.5x ~= ~13 syll/sec ~= ~525 wpm against the slow baseline but only ~2.5-3x of conversational rate — the '3.5x' headline overstates how far above normal human speech the user actually is. SYNTHETIC vs NATURAL: the headline 22 syll/sec ceiling is a formant-TTS, trained-blind result and does not transfer to compressed natural audiobooks; the realistic natural-speech sighted target is roughly 15-18 syll/sec at degraded comprehension. FIGURE VARIANCE: popular press (Scientific American) cites 25 vs 10 syll/sec; academic literature uses 22 vs 8 — same order of magnitude. EFFECT-SIZE FRAMING: the d=1.37 figure is a pre/post threshold difference, not a wpm or recall gain, and several training studies measured thresholds/verification rather than naturalistic comprehension. TIME-SENSITIVITY: the psychoacoustics is stable (2007-2023, replicated); the tool landscape is fast-moving (the extension is dated 2026) and should be re-checked.
openQuestions: What custom players or re-encoding pipelines (Rubber Band, SoundTouch, ffmpeg atempo + silence-trimming, or screen-reader TTS like NVDA/JAWS/VoiceOver) actually let a sighted user combine >3.5x rate with automatic silence-trimming and boundary-pause re-insertion in one workflow? This was not verified.

Does the time-restoration / boundary-pause benefit (validated for one-shot narrative recall in normal-hearing adults) hold up over sustained multi-hour audiobook listening, and does it net positive on total throughput once the re-inserted silence is counted (it lengthens playback time even as it raises comprehension per word)?

Is there documented evidence of any SIGHTED listener exceeding ~18 syll/sec with NATURAL compressed speech (not synthetic TTS), and what comprehension level is sustainable there versus the ~70% the trained sighted control reached with synthetic TTS at 18 syll/sec?

What do experienced blind screen-reader power users and speed-listening enthusiasts report as practical sustainable rates and training tricks (e.g., switching to formant TTS, voice selection, gradual rate ramping in a screen reader) — the practitioner/community evidence layer was not captured in the verified claims?
